author: David Huu Pham
text:
Phase 1 s1_3: Deep-dive codebase exploration findings (COMPLETE)

ENTRY POINTS (CLI Binaries)
────────────────────────────

1. bin/aura-msg (26KB, 727 lines)
   - 5 command groups: query, epoch, signal, phase, session
   - 8 implemented subcommands (8 exit patterns: 0/1/2/3)
   - Subcommands:
     · query state           → _cmd_query_state (query workflow state)
     · epoch start/cancel    → _cmd_epoch_start, _cmd_epoch_cancel (lifecycle)
     · epoch terminate       → _cmd_epoch_terminate (non-graceful kill)
     · signal vote           → _cmd_signal_vote (send review vote)
     · signal complete       → _cmd_signal_complete (slice completion)
     · phase advance         → _cmd_phase_advance (request transition)
     · session register      → _cmd_session_register (register Claude Code session)
   - Data flow: argparse → config resolution → Temporal client → workflow signal/query → formatter → stdout
   - Key patterns: asyncio, config_resolution (CLI>env>YAML>defaults), format resolution at CLI boundary

2. bin/aurad.py (10KB, 275 lines)
   - Temporal worker daemon (async)
   - Config: namespace, task_queue, server_address, audit_trail (memory|sqlite), audit_db_path
   - Registers: 3 workflows (EpochWorkflow, SliceWorkflow, ReviewPhaseWorkflow)
   - Registers: 4 activities (check_constraints, record_transition, record_audit_event, query_audit_events)
   - Audit trail initialization: InMemoryAuditTrail (dev) or SqliteAuditTrail (prod)
   - Search attributes: auto-registers via ensure_search_attributes on startup

SHARED LIBRARY: aura_protocol/ (10 files, ~360KB)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────

Types Module (types.py, 3462 lines):
  - 28+ StrEnums (all PascalCase members: PhaseId, VoteType, EventType, CmdGroup, SubCommand, etc.)
  - Key enums: PhaseId(P1-P12+Complete), Domain, RoleId(5 roles), ReviewAxis(3 axes), AuditTrailBackend(memory|sqlite), OutputFormat(json|text)
  - Frozen dataclasses: EpochInput, PhaseAdvanceSignal, ReviewVoteSignal, SessionRegisterSignal, SliceCompleteSignal
  - Type specs: PHASE_SPECS, PHASE_DOMAIN (p1→user, p8→impl, p10→impl, etc)
  - Phase parsing: 4 lookup tables (_PHASE_BY_NUMBER, _PHASE_BY_SHORT, _PHASE_BY_FULL, _PHASE_BY_NAME)
  - Audit: AuditEvent, EventType(8 event types: PhaseTransition, PhaseAdvance, VoteRecorded, etc)

Config Module (config.py, 223 lines):
  - Frozen dataclasses: ConnectionConfig, AuradConfig, AuraMsgConfig
  - Config resolution: CLI args > env vars > YAML > defaults
  - Env vars: TEMPORAL_NAMESPACE, TEMPORAL_TASK_QUEUE, TEMPORAL_ADDRESS, AURAD_AUDIT_TRAIL, AURAD_AUDIT_DB_PATH
  - YAML path: ~/.config/aura/plugins/aurad.config.yaml (sections: 'aurad' and 'aura-msg')
  - Functions: resolve_connection(), resolve_aurad_config(), resolve_aura_msg_config(), load_yaml_section()

Errors Module (errors.py, 30 lines):
  - ErrorCategory StrEnum: Connection, Workflow, Validation
  - report_error(category, what, why, impact, fix) → prints to stderr (structured format)

Formatters Module (formatters.py, 118 lines):
  - format_epoch_state(QueryStateResult, OutputFormat) → JSON or text
  - format_start_result(workflow_id, run_id, OutputFormat) → JSON or text
  - format_signal_result(success, OutputFormat) → JSON or text

Workflow Module (workflow.py, 1200+ lines):
  ┌─ Search Attributes (auto-registered via ensure_search_attributes)
  │  - SA_EPOCH_ID (text): immutable epoch identifier
  │  - SA_PHASE (keyword): current phase for forensic queries
  │  - SA_ROLE, SA_STATUS, SA_DOMAIN, SA_LAST_EVENT_TYPE (keyword)
  │
  ├─ EpochWorkflow (@workflow.defn class)
  │  Signals:
  │    • advance_phase(PhaseAdvanceSignal) → queue phase transition request
  │    • submit_vote(ReviewVoteSignal) → queue reviewer vote
  │    • slice_progress(SliceProgressSignal) → log slice progress (in-memory)
  │    • register_session(SessionRegisterSignal) → idempotent session tracking
  │  Queries:
  │    • current_state() → EpochState (mutable runtime state)
  │    • available_transitions() → list[Transition]
  │    • full_state() → QueryStateResult (CLI DTO, votes dict)
  │    • (R12 stubs: slice_progress_state, session_registry_state)
  │  Activities:
  │    • check_constraints(state, to_phase) → list[ConstraintViolation]
  │    • record_transition(TransitionRecord) → (audit stub, v1 no-op)
  │    • record_audit_event(AuditEvent) → (activity boundary)
  │    • query_audit_events(epoch_id, phase?, role?) → list[AuditEvent]
  │  Lifecycle: P1_Request → P12_Landing → Complete (deterministic loop, signal-driven)
  │  State machine: EpochStateMachine wraps phase transitions, votes, blocks, severity
  │
  ├─ SliceWorkflow (@workflow.defn)
  │  Stub: R12 (not fully wired) — will handle P9_SLICE concurrent execution
  │  Input: SliceInput (epoch_id, slice_id, phase_spec, parent_workflow_id)
  │  Output: SliceResult (success, output/error)
  │  
  └─ ReviewPhaseWorkflow (@workflow.defn)
     Stub: R12 — will handle P10_CODE_REVIEW vote-driven completion

Audit Activities (audit_activities.py, 265 lines):
  - Module-level singleton pattern: _AUDIT_TRAIL (injected before worker starts)
  - init_audit_trail(trail: AuditTrail) → DI hook
  - @activity.defn record_audit_event(event: AuditEvent) → delegates to trail
  - @activity.defn query_audit_events(epoch_id, phase?, role?) → delegates to trail
  - InMemoryAuditTrail: in-process list (test/dev)
  - execute_slice_command(command, slice_id, epoch_id) → tmux check + subprocess execution

SQLite Audit (sqlite_audit.py, 200 lines):
  - SqliteAuditTrail: aiosqlite-backed (async) durable persistence
  - ensure_schema(db_path) → creates audit_events table + indexes
  - Table: audit_events (id, epoch_id, phase, role, event_type, payload, timestamp)
  - Queries: epoch_id, phase, role filters
  - Default DB path: ~/.local/share/aura/plugin/audit.db

Interfaces (interfaces.py, 402 lines):
  - AuditTrail Protocol: record_event(AuditEvent) + query_events(...)
  - ConstraintValidatorInterface, TranscriptRecorder, SecurityGate (stubs: R12)
  - A2A content types: TextPart, FilePart, DataPart, ToolCall (camelCase JSON mapping)
  - ModelId: provider/model composite identifier (parse/str round-trip)

State Machine (state_machine.py, 250+ lines):
  - EpochState: mutable, tracks current_phase, completed_phases, review_votes, blocker_count, role, transition_history
  - TransitionRecord: frozen audit entry (from_phase, to_phase, timestamp, triggered_by, condition_met, success)
  - EpochStateMachine: wraps state, enforces phase transitions via PHASE_SPECS
  - TransitionError: raised on invalid transitions

TEMPORAL INTEGRATION POINTS
──────────────────────────

1. Client Connection
   - Client.connect(server_address, namespace=namespace) → ConnectionConfig fields
   - Default: localhost:7233, namespace='default'

2. Workflow Execution
   - client.start_workflow(EpochWorkflow.run, EpochInput(...), id=epoch_id, task_queue=task_queue)
   - client.get_workflow_handle(epoch_id) for signals/queries
   - handle.signal(EpochWorkflow.submit_vote, ReviewVoteSignal(...))
   - handle.query(EpochWorkflow.full_state) → QueryStateResult
   - handle.cancel() / handle.terminate(reason=...)

3. Worker Registration
   - Worker(client, task_queue=..., workflows=[...], activities=[...])
   - Blocks on asyncio.Event().wait() until SIGINT/SIGTERM

4. Search Attributes
   - operator_service.add_search_attributes(AddSearchAttributesRequest)
   - Indexed value types: TEXT (1), KEYWORD (2)
   - All 6 attributes registered on aurad startup

TEST STRUCTURE
──────────────

- tests/test_aura_msg.py (31KB) — CLI parsing, subprocess integration, arg routing
- tests/test_aurad.py (15KB) — worker config resolution, parse_args, env var precedence
- tests/test_formatters.py (13KB) — JSON/text output formatting
- tests/test_config.py (12KB) — config resolution logic
- tests/test_audit_activities.py (19KB) — audit trail DI, activity execution
- tests/test_sqlite_audit.py (9KB) — SQLite backend persistence
- tests/test_workflow.py — epoch state machine + transitions
- Unit tests: parse_args, config resolution, formatters
- Integration tests: subprocess invocation, Temporal client mocking (pytest-asyncio)
- Patterns: DI via kwargs, no sys.argv patching, env dict saves/restore

BUILD & PACKAGING
─────────────────

- pyproject.toml: name=aura-plugins, v0.9.9
- Dependencies: temporalio>=1.9.0, pyyaml>=6.0.3, aiosqlite>=0.22.1, jinja2>=3.1.6
- Dev deps: pytest>=8.0, pytest-asyncio>=0.24
- Package dir: scripts/aura_protocol (namespace package)
- Entry points: bin/aura-msg, bin/aurad.py (executable scripts, not setuptools console_scripts)

DATA FLOW SUMMARY (aura-msg example: query state)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. aura-msg query state --epoch-id E1 --format json --server-address localhost:7233
2. argparse.parse_args() → Namespace(group='query', subcommand='state', epoch_id='E1', ...)
3. Config resolution: CLI → env → YAML → defaults
4. Format resolution: --format arg (if set) → config.default_format
5. Route to _cmd_query_state(args, config, fmt)
6. await Client.connect(config.connection.server_address, namespace=config.connection.namespace)
7. handle = client.get_workflow_handle(args.epoch_id)
8. result = await handle.query(EpochWorkflow.full_state) → QueryStateResult
9. output = format_epoch_state(result, fmt) → JSON or text
10. print(output); return 0

ARCHITECTURAL PATTERNS
──────────────────────

1. Config DI: resolve_*_config(cli_args=..., env_dict=..., yaml_section=...) — testable, no global state
2. Audit Trail DI: init_audit_trail(trail) module-level injection — pluggable backends
3. Signal-driven workflow: state mutations queued, processed in run() loop deterministically
4. Frozen dataclasses: immutable signal/query types, safe for Temporal serialization
5. Activity boundary: non-deterministic I/O (Temporal client, SQLite, subprocess) isolated in activities
6. Search attributes: forensic queryability via namespace search (AuraEpochId, AuraPhase, etc)
7. Output formats: enum-driven (OutputFormat.Json | OutputFormat.Text), formatters are pure functions

PORTING REQUIREMENTS (Python → Go)
──────────────────────────────────

1. CLI Framework
   - flag parsing (cobra/pflag recommended for argparse-like behavior)
   - subcommand routing (cobra chains flags, subcommands elegantly)
   - help generation

2. Type Definitions (MUST port exactly)
   - 28+ StrEnums → Go iota consts or custom types with String() methods
   - Frozen dataclasses → Go structs with json tags for Temporal serialization
   - PhaseId parsing: 4 lookup tables (short, full, numeric, name) → Go map[string]PhaseId

3. Config System
   - YAML loading: gopkg.in/yaml.v3 (stdlib json for CLI args)
   - Priority chain: CLI > env > YAML > defaults
   - Env vars: TEMPORAL_NAMESPACE, TEMPORAL_TASK_QUEUE, TEMPORAL_ADDRESS, AURAD_AUDIT_TRAIL, AURAD_AUDIT_DB_PATH
   - Config file: ~/.config/aura/plugins/aurad.config.yaml

4. Temporal SDK
   - go.temporal.io/sdk (Go SDK, feature-parity with Python SDK)
   - Client.Connect(server, namespace)
   - Workflow.GetHandle(epoch_id) → Signal/Query methods
   - Worker registration (activities + workflows)
   - Search attributes API: operator_service.AddSearchAttributes

5. Output Formatting
   - JSON marshaling (encoding/json with struct tags)
   - Text: fmt.Sprintf formatters

6. Error Handling & Reporting
   - ErrorCategory enum + report_error(category, what, why, impact, fix) → stderr
   - Exit codes: 0=success, 1=validation error, 2=connection error, 3=workflow error

7. Audit Trail
   - AuditTrail interface (record_event, query_events)
   - InMemoryAuditTrail: simple slice append
   - SqliteAuditTrail: database/sql (github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3 or modernc.org/sqlite)
   - Module-level DI hook (global var + init function)

8. Data Structures to Port
   - ConnectionConfig: {namespace, task_queue, server_address}
   - AuradConfig: {connection, audit_trail, audit_db_path}
   - AuraMsgConfig: {connection, default_format}
   - Signal types: PhaseAdvanceSignal, ReviewVoteSignal, SessionRegisterSignal, SliceCompleteSignal
   - Query result: QueryStateResult (DTO for CLI output)

9. Workflow Interactions
   - Signal dispatch: handle.Signal(EpochWorkflow.SubmitVote, ReviewVoteSignal{...})
   - Query execution: handle.Query(EpochWorkflow.FullState) → QueryStateResult
   - Workflow start: client.StartWorkflow(...EpochWorkflow.Run, EpochInput{...})
   - Termination: handle.Cancel() / handle.Terminate(reason)

10. Testing Strategy
    - Unit tests: config resolution, phase parsing, type round-trips
    - Integration tests: subprocess tests (aura-msg --help, aura-msg query state with mock Temporal)
    - Temporal test client (go.temporal.io/sdk/testsuite)

DEPENDENCIES SUMMARY
────────────────────

Core:
  • go.temporal.io/sdk v1.x — Temporal client + worker
  • gopkg.in/yaml.v3 — YAML config loading
  • encoding/json — JSON marshaling (stdlib)

Optional (audit backends):
  • database/sql + go-sqlite3 or modernc.org/sqlite — SQLite audit trail

CLI:
  • github.com/spf13/cobra — Subcommand routing (recommended)
  • github.com/spf13/pflag — Flag parsing (compatible with argparse semantics)

Testing:
  • testing (stdlib) or testify/assert
  • go.temporal.io/sdk/testsuite — Temporal test utilities

KEY DECISION POINTS (Future Design)
──────────────────────────────────

1. Command Pattern: aurad.py uses simple function dispatch (dict of handlers). Go version could adopt cobra.Command hierarchy for better extensibility (user noted: 'more commands and flags eventually').

2. ACP Integration: User noted acp-go-sdk available. Alignment scope uncertain; defer to next phase. Skeleton support: SessionRegisterSignal fields (model_harness, model) suggest future Agent-Client Protocol message handling.

3. Search Attributes: Temporal Go SDK has operator_service API; may differ from Python in details (check version compatibility).

4. SliceWorkflow & ReviewPhaseWorkflow: Currently R12 stubs in Python. Porting depends on implementation status — may be bare stubs in Go until Python version completes.

File locations (repo root = /home/minttea/codebases/dayvidpham/aura-plugins/worktree/aura-protocol/):
  - bin/aura-msg
  - bin/aurad.py
  - scripts/aura_protocol/ (package dir)
  - tests/test_aura_msg.py, test_aurad.py, test_formatters.py, etc
  - pyproject.toml (deps + pytest config)

author: David Huu Pham
text:
Research findings (deep-dive s1_2): Golang port of aura-msg + aurad.

CLI Framework: Cobra recommended. Native two-level subcommand nesting maps directly to aura-msg group/subcommand pattern. First-class Viper integration solves config chain. Used by Temporal's own CLI (temporal/tctl). Kong is a viable alternative (struct-based, declarative) but lacks Viper integration.

Temporal Go SDK: Key differences from Python -- workflows are plain functions (no decorators), signals use workflow.GetSignalChannel()+Receive() instead of @workflow.signal decorators, queries use workflow.SetQueryHandler() instead of @workflow.query. Worker registration is explicit (w.RegisterWorkflow/RegisterActivity). Client API: client.Dial(), c.SignalWorkflow(), c.QueryWorkflow(), c.ExecuteWorkflow().

Command Pattern: Recommended hybrid approach -- Cobra RunE for dispatch, standalone handler functions with explicit params (no Cobra dependency) for testability. Easy refactor to full Command interface later. Maps 1:1 to current Python handler pattern.

Config Resolution: Viper natively supports CLI>env>YAML>defaults priority chain. BindPFlag, BindEnv, SetDefault, SetConfigFile cover all current resolve_*_config() logic.

Structured Errors: Custom StructuredError type implementing error interface with Report(io.Writer). Enables errors.As() extraction + testable output (writer injection vs hardcoded stderr).

Project Structure: Standard cmd/aura-msg/ + cmd/aurad/ + internal/{config,errors,handlers,formatters,types,temporal}/. Two binaries share code via internal/.

Key Risk: Signal/query name drift between client and worker -- mitigate with shared constants in internal/temporal/signals.go.

Full report: docs/research/golang-port.md

author: David Huu Pham
text:
## Polyrepo Tooling Research (s1_2 Deep Dive)

### 1. Polyrepo Management Tools Landscape

#### Tier 1: Monorepo-first tools with polyrepo capabilities

**Nx (Nrwl)** — Most active, broadest scope
- Now migrating core from TypeScript to Rust (targeting 2025-2026 completion)
- "Build Intelligence Platform" with DAG-based task orchestration, computation caching, affected-project analysis
- Polyrepo support: shared Nx plugins each repo opts into; emerging "synthetic monorepo" concept connects separate repos into a unified dependency graph
- 2026 roadmap includes expanded polyglot support and AI agent integration (MCP server for workspace context)
- Relevance to aura-plugins: HIGH for task orchestration concepts, LOW for direct adoption (JS/TS-centric, heavyweight for a Python/shell plugin ecosystem)

**Lerna** — Now maintained by Nx team
- Focused on JS/TS package publishing in monorepos
- Modern Lerna delegates task running to Nx via "useNx": true
- Not designed for polyrepo; no cross-repo capabilities
- Relevance: LOW — monorepo-only, JS-only

**Rush (Microsoft)** — Enterprise-scale monorepo
- Supports NPM/Yarn/PNPM, parallel builds, incremental builds, build caching
- Automatic changelog generation, CI integration scripts
- Strictly monorepo — no polyrepo mode
- Relevance: LOW — wrong architecture and ecosystem

**Turborepo (Vercel)** — Fast monorepo builds
- Strictly monorepo, JS/TS ecosystem
- Relevance: NONE for polyrepo

#### Tier 2: Polyrepo-native tools (bulk operations)

**multi-gitter** (github.com/lindell/multi-gitter) — Go CLI
- Runs scripts across repos in a GitHub/GitLab org, creates PRs for changes
- Supports dry-run, auto-merge after CI passes
- Good for: coordinated updates (e.g., bumping a shared dependency version across all plugin repos)
- Relevance: MEDIUM — useful for cross-repo bulk operations but no version/release orchestration

**git-xargs** (Gruntwork) — Go CLI
- Similar to multi-gitter: run commands across repos, commit + open PRs
- Concurrent execution via goroutines
- Good for: adding files, search-and-replace, upgrading configs across repos
- Relevance: MEDIUM — complementary to aura-release for cross-repo maintenance tasks

**Key difference:** multi-gitter and git-xargs solve "run X across N repos" but do NOT solve versioning, release ordering, or dependency graphs between repos.

#### Tier 3: Multi-repo coordination tools

**meta** (npm: mateodelnorte/meta) — JS CLI
- "Why choose many repos or a monolithic repo? Have both with a meta repo"
- Plugin-based: meta npm link, meta git update, meta exec
- .meta file lists all child repos; clones missing repos, runs commands across all
- Relevance: MEDIUM-HIGH — closest conceptual match to what aura-release registry does (mapping plugin names to repo paths). But JS-only, npm-centric

**mani** (github.com/alajmo/mani) — Go CLI
- YAML config (mani.yaml) listing repos + custom commands
- Run commands over all or subset of repos
- Lightweight, language-agnostic
- Relevance: MEDIUM-HIGH — clean, general-purpose. Could complement aura-release for "run aura-release --check across all registered plugins"

**myrepos / mr** (myrepos.branchable.com) — Perl
- Oldest tool in this category. Manages multiple VCS repos via ~/.mrconfig
- mr register, mr update, mr -j5 update (parallel)
- Supports any VCS (git, svn, hg, etc.)
- Relevance: LOW-MEDIUM — too generic, no release/version awareness

**Google repo** — Python
- XML manifest listing repos + revisions + branches
- Designed for AOSP (hundreds of git repos composing one product)
- mr-like checkout/sync but with pinned revisions in manifest
- Relevance: LOW — heavyweight, Android-specific workflow assumptions

#### Tier 4: Version management across packages

**Changesets** (@changesets/cli) — JS/TS
- "Intent-based" versioning: contributors declare "this PR bumps package X as minor"
- Accumulates changesets, calculates version bumps respecting inter-package dependencies
- Generates changelogs per package
- Designed for monorepos with multiple npm packages
- Relevance: MEDIUM — the intent-based model is interesting for polyrepo. aura-release already does changelog + version bumping, but lacks the "changeset file per PR" pattern that enables coordinated releases

### 2. Go-Specific Polyrepo Patterns

**Go Workspaces (go.work):**
- go.work file with `use` directives pointing at local module paths
- Enables local development across multiple modules without `replace` directives
- go.work should NOT be committed (CI should test each module independently)
- go work sync aligns dependency versions across workspace modules

**Go Module Proxy (GOPROXY/GOPRIVATE):**
- GOPRIVATE controls which modules skip the public proxy + checksum DB
- Multiple proxy fallback chain: private proxy -> proxy.golang.org -> direct
- Major version suffix convention (/v2, /v3) treats major versions as distinct modules

**Cross-repo versioning in Go:**
- Each repo is a module with its own go.mod and semver tags
- No built-in cross-repo release orchestration — you manually tag and publish
- Dependency updates are explicit (go get, go mod tidy)

### 3. Release Orchestration Across Repos

No single tool handles coordinated releases across truly independent repos well. The patterns observed:

1. **Manual coordination:** Tag repo A, update repo B's dependency, tag repo B. Most Go projects do this.
2. **Synthetic monorepo (Nx 2026):** Connect repos into a unified dependency graph, but tooling is nascent and JS-focused.
3. **Changeset-style intent files:** Each PR declares version impact; a bot aggregates and proposes releases. Requires all repos to use the same system.
4. **Registry + script (what aura-release does):** A central registry maps plugins to repos; a script walks the registry and performs operations.

### 4. Claude Code Plugin Marketplace Specifics

**marketplace.json structure:**
- Top-level: name, owner (name, email), metadata (description, version), plugins[]
- Each plugin entry: name, description, version, source (relative path or {source: "github", repo: "org/name"})
- source field supports ref (branch/tag) and sha (exact commit) for pinning

**plugin.json structure:**
- name, version, description, author, homepage, repository, license, keywords
- strict mode: when true, plugin.json is authority for component definitions

**Key metadata sync points:**
- marketplace.json metadata.version (marketplace-level version)
- marketplace.json plugins[].version (per-plugin version in marketplace listing)
- Individual plugin repo's plugin.json version
- Individual plugin repo's pyproject.toml / package.json version

### 5. Comparison: aura-release vs. Established Tools

#### What aura-release already does well:
- Auto-discovers version files (pyproject.toml, package.json, plugin.json, marketplace.json) — no tool surveyed does this generically
- SemVer parsing + bumping with consistency checking across manifests
- Changelog generation from conventional commits
- Git commit + tag automation with rollback on failure
- Plugin registry with marketplace awareness (init/add/list/remove)
- Clean working tree enforcement, detached HEAD guard
- Extensible _ScanSpec pattern for adding new file types

#### Gaps that established tools fill:

| Gap | Tool(s) that address it |
|-----|------------------------|
| Cross-repo bulk operations (run command across all plugin repos) | multi-gitter, git-xargs, mani, meta |
| Dependency graph between plugins (release ordering) | Nx (synthetic monorepo), Changesets |
| Intent-based versioning (changeset files per PR) | Changesets |
| Parallel repo sync/clone/update | mani, meta, myrepos, Google repo |
| CI/CD pipeline integration for multi-repo releases | Nx, Rush (monorepo-only though) |
| Version pinning with exact commit SHA across repos | Google repo manifest, go.mod |

#### Gaps that NO surveyed tool fills well:
- Python/shell plugin ecosystem versioning (all major tools are JS/TS-centric)
- Claude Code marketplace.json sync (aura-release is the only tool aware of this format)
- Polyrepo release ordering with a dependency DAG (tools either assume monorepo or don't do release orchestration)

### 6. Recommendation: Extend aura-release, don't replace it

**Rationale:**
1. aura-release already handles the hardest part: marketplace.json-aware versioning with multi-manifest consistency. No off-the-shelf tool understands this format.
2. The polyrepo gap is primarily "run operations across repos" — this is the simplest part to add.
3. The registry already exists as the coordination point. Extending it with:
   - `aura-release registry exec <cmd>` (run command in each registered plugin repo)
   - `aura-release registry check-all` (version consistency across all plugins)
   - `aura-release registry release-order` (topological sort if inter-plugin deps exist)
   would close the main gaps without introducing a new tool dependency.
4. For bulk cross-repo operations beyond release (CI config updates, shared config sync), mani is the best complement — lightweight, language-agnostic, YAML config.

**What NOT to adopt:**
- Nx/Lerna/Rush/Turborepo — monorepo-first, JS-only, massive dependency footprint
- Changesets — interesting model but npm-centric; the intent-based pattern could be replicated in aura-release if needed
- Google repo — too heavyweight, wrong abstraction level

**Potential additions to aura-release (prioritized):**
1. `registry exec` — run arbitrary command across all plugin repos (like mani/meta but using existing registry)
2. `registry sync-versions` — update marketplace.json plugin entries to match each plugin repo's current version
3. `registry release-order` — if inter-plugin dependencies exist, compute and display topological release order
4. Consider adopting mani as a complementary tool for non-release cross-repo operations

Review task: aura-plugins-8xhrw

The proposal is structurally clean and idiomatically Go. The pkg/protocol vs internal/ split is correct and mirrors the reference repo pattern. Handler functions are CLI-framework-agnostic (testable). DI via struct replaces the Python global singleton appropriately. Signal/query constants prevent name drift at compile time. StructuredError is testable via io.Writer without over-engineering. The wave schedule has no false dependencies.

Minor non-blocking observations: PasturedConfig.AuditTrail should use the typed AuditTrailBackend enum rather than string; SemVer.Bump could use a typed BumpKind; PluginRegistry.Exec mixes command execution into a data type (acceptable for MVP). None are blockers.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE

## Axis: Correctness — Does the proposal faithfully address all R1-R7 requirements?

### What is Correct

R1 (pastured): Fully addressed — EpochWorkflow/SliceWorkflow/ReviewPhaseWorkflow, 4 activities, InMemory+SQLite audit trail, search attribute auto-registration, Viper config, all 4 signal constants.

R2 (pasture-msg): Fully addressed — all 8 subcommands, Cobra RunE → standalone handlers (D4), StructuredError with 6-field reporting, OutputFormat, exit codes 0/1/2/3 confirmed in BDD.

R3 (pasture-release): Fully addressed — SemVer, VersionFile, changelog, git helpers, PluginRegistry with 3 new methods (Exec, SyncVersions, ReleaseOrder).

R5 (Shared Go library): Covered via internal/ packages + pkg/protocol/ convergence types.

R7 (Polyrepo marketplace): Covered via S11/S12 and PluginRegistry.

---

### Issues Requiring Revision

**ISSUE-1 (BLOCKER): /pasture:install-cli skill is a URD R6 MVP gate with no plan**

R6 explicitly names 4 distribution channels including '/pasture:install-cli skill: Claude Code skill for OS/arch detection + binary fetch'. The proposal's validation checklist mentions it, but there is no structural element: no cmd/, no skills/ directory, no interface or file stub. This is one of 4 user-stated MVP-gated distribution channels. It needs at minimum a placement (e.g., skills/pasture/install-cli/) and a structural description in the proposal even if S12 carries the implementation.

**ISSUE-2 (BLOCKER): PhaseId type duplication — internal/types vs pkg/protocol — no aliasing strategy**

internal/types/enums.go defines PhaseId and the handler signatures use types.PhaseId. pkg/protocol/types.go also defines PhaseId. These are two distinct Go types — assignment or comparison between them will fail to compile. The proposal provides no aliasing (e.g., type PhaseId = protocol.PhaseId in internal/types), no conversion function, and no canonical direction. Workers building S2 and S5 will hit this immediately. Fix: either (a) have internal/types alias pkg/protocol types (preferred — public is canonical), or (b) remove PhaseId from internal/types entirely and import pkg/protocol directly.

**ISSUE-3 (IMPORTANT): Wave 4 parallelism claim is incorrect for S12**

The proposal states 'Wave 4 can run in parallel with Waves 2-3 since pasture-release is independent.' S11 (pasture-release) is independent of Waves 2-3 and can run in parallel. S12 (Distribution) explicitly depends on S5 and S8, both of which are Wave 2-3 items. S12 cannot run parallel to Waves 2-3 — it must wait. The note should say 'S11 can run in parallel with Waves 2-3; S12 must wait for S5 and S8.'

**ISSUE-4 (IMPORTANT): Hooks interface (R4/D7) has no structural representation**

R4 states 'Claude Code hooks interface: plan integration points for hook-based event capture.' URD D7 confirms: 'Hooks interface for Claude Code integration (user-stated requirement).' The proposal has acp/client.go, acp/adapter_claude.go, and SharedIndexer — but no hooks package, no interface definition, not even a file stub or note describing where hooks will integrate. This is a user-stated MVP requirement in R4. The proposal should show where hooks land (e.g., internal/hooks/ or as part of internal/acp/) and what the planned interface looks like.

**ISSUE-5 (MINOR): SemVer.Bump takes stringly-typed kind string**

func (v SemVer) Bump(kind string) SemVer uses 'major'/'minor'/'patch' as magic strings. Given the project's preference for strongly-typed enums (design principles stated in CLAUDE.md), this should be a BumpKind type (e.g., type BumpKind string with const BumpMajor, BumpMinor, BumpPatch). Low risk but inconsistent with stated design principles.

---

### Summary

Two blockers prevent ratification: the /pasture:install-cli skill (a user-stated MVP distribution channel) is entirely unplanned in the proposal structure, and the PhaseId duplication between internal/types and pkg/protocol is a compile-time correctness hazard that will block workers. Both are fixable with targeted additions to the proposal without requiring architectural changes.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

## Axis: Correctness — Reviewer A (Round 1)

### Prior Finding Resolution — All Verified

**A-BLOCKER-1 (PhaseId duplication): RESOLVED**
PROPOSAL-2 establishes a clear canonical strategy: PhaseId and EventType are defined once in pkg/protocol/types.go. internal/types/aliases.go declares type aliases (type PhaseId = protocol.PhaseId) — these are identical Go types, not distinct types. Constant re-exports follow the same pattern. Assignment, comparison, and function calls across package boundaries will compile without conversion. The compile-time hazard from PROPOSAL-1 is eliminated.

**A-BLOCKER-2 (/pasture:install-cli skill placement): RESOLVED**
skills/install-cli/SKILL.md is now present in the project structure with a full behavioral description (OS/arch detection, GitHub API release resolution, binary download, install path, verification, and fallback). S12 (Distribution) includes it in scope. R6 now has a structural home in the project layout.

**A-IMPORTANT-1 (Wave schedule S12): RESOLVED**
The wave schedule is corrected. S11 (pasture-release) is now labeled Wave 3.5 and runs parallel with Waves 2-3. S12 (Distribution) is now Wave 4 alone, correctly reflecting its dependency on S5, S8, and S11. The prior incorrect claim that 'Wave 4 can run in parallel with Waves 2-3' is no longer present.

**A-IMPORTANT-2 (Hooks interface): RESOLVED**
internal/hooks/ package is now fully defined with HookEvent typed enum (7 constants), HookPayload struct, HookHandler interface (Handle + Events methods), and Manager (Register + Dispatch). The non-blocking dispatch contract is stated explicitly and mirrored in the Key Risk Mitigations table. The BDD section includes two hooks scenarios. R4 hook integration points now have structural representation.

**A-MINOR (BumpKind): RESOLVED**
SemVer.Bump(kind types.BumpKind) SemVer now uses the typed BumpKind enum (BumpMajor/BumpMinor/BumpPatch defined in internal/types/enums.go). Consistent with the project's stated preference for strongly-typed enums.

---

### R1-R7 Completeness Check

**R1 (pastured): PASS**
EpochWorkflow, SliceWorkflow, ReviewPhaseWorkflow, 4 activities (check_constraints, record_transition, record_audit_event, query_audit_events), InMemory+SQLite audit trail (AuditTrailBackend enum), EnsureSearchAttributes, Viper config, all 4 signal names in shared constants, PasturedConfig with typed AuditTrailBackend. BDD covers startup, constraint violation, and audit durability (kill-restart-query).

**R2 (pasture-msg): PASS**
All 8 subcommands have standalone handler functions with typed signatures. Exit code table is explicit (0/1/2/3 — note: the proposal maps 1=workflow, 2=connection, 3=validation; the BDD text aligns with this). All 8 subcommands have BDD scenarios. Config resolution BDD covers all four priority levels (CLI > env > YAML > default). StructuredError + ErrorCategory enum present. OutputFormat enum present.

**R3 (pasture-release): PASS**
SemVer with ParseSemVer/Bump(BumpKind)/String, VersionFile interface (Read/Write/Name/Path), DiscoverVersionFiles, changelog.go, git.go, PluginRegistry with Exec/SyncVersions/ReleaseOrder, runner.go with documented refactor path. All 3 new features (registry exec, sync-versions, release-order) have BDD scenarios.

**R4 (ACP Integration): PASS**
acp-go-sdk imported, SessionHandler interface, Client struct implementing acp.Client, SharedIndexer ([]SessionUpdate → []SessionEntry), AdapterClaude (JSONL), AdapterOpenCode (JSON), HandleSessionEnd scenario. BDD covers 1000-update throughput, ToolCallUpdate field mapping, session termination, malformed input. Hooks interface now present for Claude Code integration.

**R5 (Shared Go library): PASS**
internal/ packages covering config, errors, types, temporal, formatters, hooks, audit, acp, release. Shared signal/query constants in internal/temporal/constants.go prevent name drift. pkg/protocol/ provides public convergence types.

**R6 (Build & Distribution — 4 channels): PASS**
(1) Nix flake: flake.nix + nix build BDD scenario. (2) GitHub Releases: .github/workflows/release.yml + CI BDD scenario for 4 platforms. (3) /pasture:install-cli skill: skills/install-cli/SKILL.md with full behavior description + BDD scenario. (4) go install: mentioned in validation checklist. All 4 channels addressed.

**R7 (Polyrepo Marketplace): PASS**
Separate pasture repo established, PluginRegistry extends pasture-release with exec/sync-versions/release-order, marketplace.json coordination documented. S11 and S12 cover scope.

---

### New Correctness Issues in PROPOSAL-2

**No new blockers identified.**

One observation worth noting (non-blocking):

The exit code mapping in the BDD section reverses codes 1 and 2 compared to the exit code table:
- Exit code table: 1=workflow/application error, 2=connection error, 3=validation error
- BDD connection scenario: 'exits 2' with category=connection
This is consistent — the BDD correctly uses 2 for connection errors matching the table. No issue here.

The Manager.Dispatch contract says 'runs handlers concurrently with context timeout' but the HookHandler.Handle contract says 'Implementations must be non-blocking.' These are slightly in tension: if Dispatch calls handlers concurrently via goroutines, then Handle can technically block. The intent is clear (caller is non-blocking), but the implementation note could be clarified. This is a minor documentation note, not a correctness blocker — the goroutine+timeout pattern correctly achieves the non-blocking dispatch goal.

---

### End-User Alignment

The end-user (aura-protocol developer) needs: a Go binary that replaces aurad + aura-msg, can be installed without Go or Nix, connects to Temporal, sends all protocol commands, and observes live agent sessions. PROPOSAL-2 faithfully covers all of these. The install-cli skill addresses the end-user need for tool adoption without build toolchain requirements. ACP full client addresses live observability. Config resolution is explicit and testable.

---

### Verdict

VOTE: ACCEPT

All 5 PROPOSAL-1 Reviewer A findings are resolved. R1-R7 are fully covered. The proposal is internally consistent. No new correctness blockers introduced.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

Axis: Elegance — Is the design appropriately complex for the problem?

## Summary

PROPOSAL-2 resolves all three notes from my PROPOSAL-1 review. The design is well-organized, appropriately scoped, and does not over-engineer for an MVP of this complexity.

## Strengths

**Structural organization:** The pkg/ vs internal/ split is textbook Go idiom. Public convergence types in pkg/protocol/ with type aliases in internal/types/ (not copies) is exactly right — single source of truth, zero conversion overhead, no drift.

**Separation of concerns:** handlers/ decouples Temporal interactions from Cobra; formatters/ is cleanly separate from business logic; hooks/ has a narrow interface with self-declaring event subscriptions (Events() []HookEvent) — idiomatic and avoids a registration table. The acp/ boundary (internal implementation, public types in pkg/protocol/) reflects a well-documented tradeoff (T6).

**Complexity proportionality:** The problem is genuinely complex (concurrent workflows, distributed state machine, live ACP streaming, polyrepo release management). The proposal does not punch above its weight — no plugin systems, no generics gymnastics, no event bus. Each interface is narrow. PluginRegistry.Exec acknowledged with a documented refactor path (RegistryRunner) — correct MVP call.

**StructuredError design:** Report(io.Writer) is the right Go idiom — testable via bytes.Buffer, composable, no global state. What/Why/Impact/Fix maps cleanly to actionable-error requirements.

**ACP scope:** SessionHandler (two-method interface), SharedIndexer (single Index method), and adapter free functions are all minimal and appropriate for pure transformations.

## Minor observations (non-blocking)

1. **Enum value style inconsistency:** ErrorCategory values use 'connection error' (lowercase, two words); HookEvent uses snake_case; EventType uses PascalCase; OutputFormat uses lowercase single words. Four different conventions across typed string enums. Cosmetic, does not affect correctness or testability.

2. **Exit code ordering discrepancy:** URD says 1=validation, 2=connection, 3=workflow. Proposal exit code table says 1=workflow, 2=connection, 3=validation. The BDD scenarios are self-consistent, so this is documentation noise, not a runtime flaw. Should be harmonized before implementation to avoid confusion.

Neither observation warrants REVISE. Both are documentation-level cleanup items.

## PROPOSAL-1 notes — confirmed resolved

- C-note (AuditTrail config stringly-typed): PasturedConfig.AuditTrail now uses AuditTrailBackend typed enum. Resolved.
- C-note (BumpKind stringly-typed): BumpKind typed enum introduced in internal/types/enums.go. Resolved.
- C-note (Exec mixes concerns): Acknowledged, documented RegistryRunner refactor path. Acceptable for MVP. Resolved.

All three PROPOSAL-1 notes resolved. Two minor documentation observations (enum value style inconsistency, exit code ordering between URD and proposal) noted as non-blocking cleanup items. Design is appropriately complex, well-organized, and correctly scoped for MVP.

text:
VOTE: REVISE

## Reviewer B — Test Quality Axis — Round 1

### F1–F7 Resolution Status (from PROPOSAL-1 findings)

All seven prior findings have been addressed in PROPOSAL-2:

- F1 (6/8 subcommands missing BDD): All 8 subcommands now have BDD scenarios with correct exit codes per the proposal's own table. EXIT CODE TABLE CONFLICT (see new finding below).
- F2 (Config resolution BDD): A complete 4-step layered BDD scenario is present (CLI > env > YAML > defaults with explicit fallback assertion). RESOLVED.
- F3 (ACP adapter coverage): AdapterClaude, AdapterOpenCode, SharedIndexer ToolCallUpdate, HandleSessionEnd, and malformed input cases all have scenarios. RESOLVED.
- F4 (Back-pressure untestable): Replaced with concrete '1000 updates in sequence, no dropped entries' metric. RESOLVED.
- F5 (2/3 release features no BDD): sync-versions and release-order both have explicit scenarios. RESOLVED.
- F6 (Audit durability unexecutable): Rewritten as kill-restart-query sequence with SQLite backend. RESOLVED.
- F7 (Coverage thresholds missing): Thresholds now cover all key packages (temporal 70%, audit 75%, acp 70%, errors 90%, hooks 80%, release 75%, protocol 90%). RESOLVED.

### NEW FINDING — BLOCKER: Exit Code Table Conflicts with URD R2

The PROPOSAL-2 exit code table defines:

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | Workflow/application error |
| 2 | Connection error |
| 3 | Validation error (bad input) |

The URD (aura-plugins-jbnx3) R2 states explicitly:
> Exit codes: 0=success, 1=validation, 2=connection, 3=workflow

The Python implementation being ported (bin/aura-msg) uses:
> 2=connection error, 3=workflow error (phase advance also uses 1=validation)

The proposal's exit code table has codes 1 and 3 swapped relative to the URD. The BDD scenarios are internally consistent with the proposal's own (incorrect) table — for example, the signal vote scenario correctly uses exit 3 for 'invalid axis' (which is a validation error by the proposal's table). But this means every BDD scenario exercising validation error exit codes is testing behavior that contradicts the user's stated requirement.

This is not a cosmetic issue. Exit codes are part of the public API contract for shell scripting, CI integration, and any automation that wraps pasture-msg. Implementing the wrong exit code semantics will silently break downstream integrators.

**Actionable fix:** Align the PROPOSAL-2 exit code table with the URD R2 definition (1=validation, 2=connection, 3=workflow). Update all BDD scenarios that reference exit codes 1 and 3 accordingly. The phase advance handler in the Python source already uses this convention for validation errors (return 1 on bad phase ID).

### Remaining Positive Observations

- The BDD scenarios are now genuinely executable as written — each has a concrete Given/When/Then/Should Not structure with observable outcomes.
- Coverage thresholds are well-calibrated: higher for pure-logic packages (errors, types, protocol at 90%) and lower for Temporal-integration packages where mocking is harder (temporal, audit at 70–75%).
- The config resolution scenario covers all 4 priority levels with separate When clauses — this is exactly what is needed to catch Viper wiring bugs.
- The 1000-update ACP scenario is now concrete and automatable.
- The audit durability scenario is now truly executable (SIGKILL, not just 'process restart').
- The hooks non-blocking scenario correctly tests the 'unsubscribed event' case which is a common edge case.

### Summary

All 7 prior findings are resolved. One new BLOCKER finding on exit code table inconsistency with the URD. A targeted fix to align the exit code table and update the affected BDD scenarios will clear this review.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

## Reviewer B — Test Quality Axis — Round 2

### Verification Scope

This round focused exclusively on verifying the Round 1 BLOCKER: exit code table swapped (1↔3) vs URD R2. All prior F1-F7 findings were confirmed resolved in Round 1 and are not re-examined here.

### Exit Code Table — VERIFIED CORRECT

The exit code table (lines 685-690) now reads:

| Exit Code | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | Validation error (bad input) |
| 2 | Connection error |
| 3 | Workflow/application error |

This matches URD R2 exactly: '0=success, 1=validation, 2=connection, 3=workflow'. The BLOCKER is resolved.

### BDD Scenario Exit Code Audit — All 6 References Verified

Every BDD scenario containing an explicit exit code reference was checked against the corrected table:

1. epoch start — duplicate workflow → exits 3, category=workflow. CORRECT (3=workflow).
2. epoch cancel — already cancelled → exits 3. CORRECT (3=workflow).
3. signal vote — invalid axis name → exits 1, category=validation. CORRECT (1=validation).
4. phase advance — invalid phase string → exits 1, category=validation. CORRECT (1=validation).
5. session register — empty session-id or role → exits 1. CORRECT (1=validation, implicit).
6. No Temporal server → exits 2, category=connection. CORRECT (2=connection).

All 6 BDD exit code references are internally consistent with the corrected table. No missed references. No residual 1↔3 swaps.

### No New Findings

No new test quality issues were identified in this review pass. The BDD scenarios remain executable, the coverage thresholds remain well-calibrated, and the observable outcome structure is intact from Round 1.

### Summary

The Round 1 BLOCKER is fully resolved. The exit code table and all BDD scenarios now faithfully implement the URD R2 contract. All prior F1-F7 findings remain resolved.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE

Summary of findings:

BLOCKER (0): None. Build passes, go vet passes, all tests pass including with -race.

IMPORTANT (3):
1. aura-plugins-cmdos — InMemoryAuditTrail in temporal/activities.go has no mutex. RecordEvent appends to a slice and QueryEvents iterates it without synchronization. Temporal workers invoke activities from multiple goroutines concurrently; this is a data race. The parallel implementation in internal/audit/memory.go correctly uses sync.RWMutex.

2. aura-plugins-9mzpk — EpochWorkflow.Run directly mutates EpochStateMachine internals (TransitionHistory, LastError) on the Advance error path, violating the documented API contract that callers must not modify the pointer returned by State(). Should be encapsulated behind a new state machine method.

3. aura-plugins-q9udd — RecordTransition activity writes an audit event with EpochID set to the empty string (acknowledged in a code comment). These events are unqueryable by epoch ID. The workflow also separately calls RecordAuditEvent with the correct epochID, making the RecordTransition audit write either redundant or broken, depending on intent.

MINOR (2):
4. aura-plugins-49j5d — dispatchErrors.Error() constructs a msgs []string with individual error details but discards it, returning only a count string. Dead code; hides diagnostic info from callers.

5. aura-plugins-js1e8 — viper.go Viper default for audit_trail is 'sqlite', but the CLI flag default says 'memory'. Since bindChangedFlag only forwards explicitly-set flags, 'sqlite' silently wins on an unconfigured run, contradicting the help text.

Voting REVISE because IMPORTANT findings #1 (potential data race) and #3 (audit trail correctness) need to be addressed before landing.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE

## Review Summary

Reviewed all 76 Go files across 13 packages in pasture/. The overall design quality is high: the hybrid command pattern (D4) is faithfully implemented with truly standalone handler functions, D10 shared constants prevent drift for EpochWorkflow signals, D11 is honored (no aliases.go — pkg/protocol imported directly), D12 Adapter interface is extensible, D13 mixed enum styles are context-appropriate, and D14 exit codes are consistently mapped through ExitCode().

However, two BLOCKER issues require revision before the code can proceed.

## BLOCKER-1: Duplicate AuditTrail interface (aura-plugins-3lsrq)

internal/temporal/activities.go defines its own AuditTrail interface and InMemoryAuditTrail that are structurally identical to internal/audit's Trail and InMemoryAuditTrail. This creates two parallel hierarchies for a single concept, forces pastured/main.go to bridge between them, and means any new backend implementor must satisfy two separate interfaces.

Fix: internal/temporal/activities.go should import and use audit.Trail directly. Remove the temporal.AuditTrail interface and temporal.InMemoryAuditTrail entirely.

## BLOCKER-2: ReleaseOptions.BumpKind is stringly-typed (aura-plugins-33ep8)

internal/release/runner.go declares BumpKind as string and re-implements the BumpKind switch in bumpFromString(), bypassing types.BumpKind entirely. This directly contradicts D13 and proposal change #5 which introduced BumpKind precisely to avoid this.

Fix: Change ReleaseOptions.BumpKind to types.BumpKind. Remove bumpFromString. Parse the CLI string at the CLI boundary in cmd/pasture-release/main.go.

## IMPORTANT findings (5 total — do not block, but should be addressed in follow-up)

- aura-plugins-e2ogu: TemporalClient.ExecuteWorkflow uses interface{} for options
- aura-plugins-c7xg9: EpochWorkflow.Run directly mutates state machine state pointer
- aura-plugins-7vdpw: EnsureSearchAttributes hardcodes namespace 'default'
- aura-plugins-9l6rb: dispatchErrors.Error() discards individual handler error details
- aura-plugins-1ryhm: SliceWorkflow uses stringly-typed signal names not in constants.go

## MINOR findings (3 total)

- aura-plugins-60qyz: config_types.go is an empty placeholder file
- aura-plugins-9okt1: ContentBlock has ambiguous dual text fields
- aura-plugins-1rlwa: PhaseId wire values differ from proposal spec without documentation

## What is strong

- Go idioms are well applied: errors.As in ExitCode(), io.Writer in Report(), zero-value usefulness in InMemoryAuditTrail, generic queryWorkflow[T any] in handlers.
- Package boundaries are clean with no circular dependencies.
- Naming is idiomatic with no stuttering.
- The state machine, Adapter registry, hooks Manager, and SharedIndexer are all extensible without modification.
- Consistency is high across all 8 handler functions.
- Error messages throughout are actionable with what/why/impact/fix fields.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

## Cycle 2 Re-Review — Elegance & Design

All 2 BLOCKERs and all 5 IMPORTANTs from cycle 1 are confirmed fixed. No new design issues found.

### BLOCKER-1 verification: Duplicate AuditTrail interface

internal/temporal/activities.go now imports audit.Trail directly (line 11) and uses the module-level `var auditTrail audit.Trail` singleton. The temporal.AuditTrail interface and temporal.InMemoryAuditTrail types are gone entirely. The bridge in pastured/main.go is no longer needed. Fix is clean and idiomatic.

### BLOCKER-2 verification: Stringly-typed BumpKind

internal/release/runner.go declares `ReleaseOptions.BumpKind` as `types.BumpKind` (line 17). The `bumpFromString()` helper is removed. The switch at line 123 branches on `types.BumpMajor`, `types.BumpMinor`, `types.BumpPatch` — typed constants from the canonical enum. The CLI boundary parse belongs in cmd/pasture-release/main.go, which is the correct location. Fix is clean.

### IMPORTANT fixes verified

1. ExecuteWorkflow signature (aura-plugins-e2ogu): handlers/client.go line 24 declares `options client.StartWorkflowOptions` directly — not interface{}. Typed, compile-time safe. Confirmed.

2. RecordFailedTransition (aura-plugins-c7xg9): state_machine.go lines 324-341 introduce a clean method on EpochStateMachine. workflow.go line 193 calls `w.sm.RecordFailedTransition(...)` — direct mutation of State() pointer is gone. The comment on line 192 explicitly documents the invariant. Well-designed.

3. EnsureSearchAttributes namespace parameter (aura-plugins-7vdpw): search_attributes.go line 75 accepts a `namespace string` parameter. The OperatorServiceProvider interface (lines 51-53) is narrow and idiomatic — only one method, satisfies the Go 'accept interfaces, return concretes' idiom. The compile-time assertion on line 137 (`var _ OperatorServiceProvider = (client.Client)(nil)`) prevents drift. Confirmed.

4. dispatchErrors.Error() (aura-plugins-9l6rb): hooks/hooks.go lines 223-231 build the `msgs` slice and join it correctly. Each handler error is enumerated with [N] prefix. The Unwrap() method (line 235) exposes the error slice for errors.As/Is compatibility. Confirmed.

5. SliceWorkflow signal constants (aura-plugins-1ryhm): workflow_slice.go lines 70 and 78 reference `SignalStartSlice` and `SignalCompleteSlice` from constants.go (lines 22 and 24). No stringly-typed literals remain. Confirmed.

### No new issues introduced

The fixes are targeted and surgical. No new coupling, no new abstractions, no regressions. All 11 packages build cleanly (`go build ./...`). All tests pass (`go test ./...`), including temporal workflow tests and handler mock tests.

The design quality that was strong in cycle 1 — typed enums, narrow interfaces, zero-value usefulness, generic queryWorkflow[T], consistent error structure — is preserved.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE

## Cycle 2 Re-Review — Test Quality

### What was verified

All 5 IMPORTANT findings from cycle 1 were addressed. Tests run clean (13 packages, all pass). Most coverage numbers improved significantly:

- internal/temporal: 73.1% → 82.4% (+9.3%) — new query handler tests + EnsureSearchAttributes + UninitializedTrail tests are substantive and test observable behavior
- internal/handlers: 80.4% → 81.8% (+1.4%)

The new tests are behaviorally meaningful, not padding:
- search_attributes_test.go: 5 tests exercise real paths through EnsureSearchAttributes via a narrow OperatorServiceProvider mock — correctly mocks the dependency, not the system under test
- TestEpochWorkflow_QueryAvailableTransitions and TestEpochWorkflow_QueryFullState: invoke live query handlers through Temporal's test env, assert concrete field values
- TestRecordAuditEvent_UninitializedTrail: triggers the nil-guard code path and verifies error is returned
- InitAuditTrail singleton concern from cycle 1 is resolved — temporal package no longer owns its own InMemoryAuditTrail

### New finding: IMPORTANT

**aura-plugins-agxjf** — hooks.dispatchErrors.Error() fix breaks coverage threshold

The fix to dispatchErrors.Error() (commit 5d0f3c9) correctly replaced dead code with a real multi-line message builder, but no test was added to exercise it. Result: internal/hooks coverage dropped from 81.1% → 75.0%, below the 80% threshold.

What to add in hooks_test.go:
1. Register two handlers on the same event, both returning distinct errors
2. Call Dispatch() → capture err
3. Assert err.Error() contains "[1]" and "[2]" (numbered lines from the new format)
4. Assert errors.Unwrap(err) returns both original errors

This would bring dispatchErrors.Error() and Unwrap() from 0% to covered, restoring the package above threshold.

### Unchanged findings (pre-existing, not regressions)

- hooks.dispatchErrors.Error() / Unwrap() at 0% (same as cycle 1, now a regression due to added code)
- audit/memory.go Events() shows 0% in per-package run but IS covered by temporal_test package — not a real gap
- DefaultClientFactory and realClient wrapper methods at 0% (intentional, integration-only)

### Verdict

One new IMPORTANT: coverage threshold regression in internal/hooks introduced by the cycle 1 fix. The fix was semantically correct but needs a test for the new multi-line error path before this clears.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

## Cycle 3 Re-Review — Hooks Test Fix

### What was verified

1. **New tests in internal/hooks/hooks_test.go:**
   - `TestDispatchErrors_Error_MultipleHandlers`: Exercises the multi-error path in `Error()` at lines 223-231 (fmt.Sprintf formatting + loop). Asserts numbered prefix [1], [2] and both error messages are present.
   - `TestDispatchErrors_Unwrap`: Verifies that `errors.Is()` finds both wrapped errors via the `Unwrap() []error` method. Tests the error chain traversal contract required for proper error handling in callers.

2. **Coverage verification:**
   - internal/hooks: 97.5% total coverage (well above 80% threshold)
   - Error() method: 88.9% (multi-error path now covered; single-error case at line 220-221 is reasonable omission)
   - Unwrap() method: 100.0%

3. **Test quality:**
   - Both tests are behaviorally meaningful, not padding
   - Realistic scenario: two handlers both fail on the same event
   - Observable outcomes: error message structure and error chain traversal work correctly
   - No brittle assertions (e.g., timing-based); purely logical verification

### Verdict

The hooks test fix successfully addresses the Cycle 2 IMPORTANT finding. Coverage threshold is restored. The new tests verify the error aggregation contract that callers (aurad daemon, CLI) depend on.

ACCEPT — ready to merge.

User verbatim on Pipeline: 'Should just be something like a Pipeline. The PhaseId = name, and their index in the slice determines their pX'

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

All 5 UAT revisions verified correct. Toolchain results: go build ./... clean, go vet ./... clean, go test ./... all pass (12 packages), go test -race ./... all pass.

Revision-by-revision findings:

1. EventType PascalCase (pkg/protocol/types.go): All 8 EventType values are PascalCase (PhaseTransition, PhaseAdvance, VoteRecorded, ConstraintChecked, SliceStarted, SliceCompleted, SessionRegistered, ReviewCycleStarted). No snake_case EventType values found anywhere — the snake_case instances in hooks.go are the separate HookEvent type (wire format, correct by design).

2. Exit code 4 for CategoryConfig (internal/errors/errors.go): ExitCode() returns 4 for CategoryConfig. TestExitCode_Config explicitly asserts got != 4 and passes. Full exit code table verified: Validation=1, Connection=2, Workflow=3, Config=4, unknown=1, nil=0.

3. Configurable hook dispatch timeout (internal/hooks/hooks.go): Manager has dispatchTimeout field. ManagerOption functional option type defined. WithDispatchTimeout(d) implemented with guard (d > 0 required, else default used). NewManager(opts ...ManagerOption) accepts zero args with 5s default. Dispatch() uses m.dispatchTimeout for context.WithTimeout.

4. Static ACP adapter registry (internal/acp/adapter.go): No sync.RWMutex. No RegisterAdapter function. Static compile-time map with 'claude-jsonl' and 'opencode-json' entries. GetAdapter reads from immutable map. Correct error message lists registered formats and points to the static registry for adding new adapters.

5. PhaseId Pipeline pattern (pkg/protocol/types.go): All 13 PhaseId values are name-only (no 'pX-' prefix). Pipeline type implemented with PhaseNumber, PhaseAt, Contains, Next, Index methods. DefaultPipeline holds 12 phases (PhaseComplete excluded). ParsePhaseId handles name-only, pX, pX-name (legacy), and number-only formats. Tests for legacy pX-name format confirm backward compatibility is maintained.

author: David Huu Pham
text: SLICE-1 complete: Extended Trail interface with RecordSessionEntries/QuerySessionEntries. InMemoryAuditTrail uses RWMutex-protected slice. SqliteAuditTrail adds session_entries table (24 columns, idx_session_id index) with transactional batch INSERT. author: David Huu Pham
text:
SLICE-2 complete: Hooks singleton + workflow hook dispatch implemented.

## Summary

### L1 — Types (aura-plugins-oej6w)

  - InitHooksManager(mgr *Manager) — injects singleton, nil resets state
  - GetManager() *Manager — returns singleton (nil if uninitialized)

### L2 — Tests (aura-plugins-3ui63)

  - TestInitHooksManager_SetAndGet, TestGetManager_BeforeInit_ReturnsNil
  - TestInitHooksManager_NilResetsState, TestInitHooksManager_CanReplace
  - TestDispatchHook_NilManager_IsNoOp (two variants)
  - TestDispatchHook_WithManager_DispatchesToHandler + HandlerError + MultipleHandlers
  - TestDispatchHook_HookPhaseTransition_CorrectEvent
  - TestDispatchHook_HookConstraintViolation_CorrectEvent
  - TestDispatchHook_SliceLifecycle_AllThreeEvents
  - TestDispatchHook_ContextCancelled_NilManager_IsNoOp

### L3 — Impl (aura-plugins-7jmp1)

- activities.go: CheckConstraints fires HookConstraintViolation when violations found (best-effort)

### Prerequisites fixed

## Quality Gates
