author: David Huu Pham
text: SLICE-3 complete: IndexingSessionHandler (internal/acp/session_handler.go) accumulates updates per session, fires HookSessionStarted on first update, flushes via SharedIndexer+Trail.RecordSessionEntries on session end, fires HookSessionEnded, exposes EntriesRecorded(). RunAgentSessionInput/Result types + RunAgentSession Temporal activity added to internal/temporal/activities.go. Registered in RegisterWorkflows (workflow.go). cmd/pastured/main.go now calls hooks.InitHooksManager(hooksMgr). 10 parallel tests in session_handler_test.go. go build ./... succeeds; go test ./... passes all packages. Commit: 3b729ca.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
IMPORTANT-1: RunAgentSessionResult.SessionID and StopReason are declared but never populated.

What: RunAgentSessionResult has SessionID and StopReason fields that are always empty strings in the returned result.
Where: internal/temporal/activities.go lines 246-256 (struct), lines 348-364 (RunAgentSession return path)
Why: The code block at lines 356-361 logs the session count but never queries acpClient.SessionStats() or any data source to populate SessionID or StopReason in the result struct.
Impact: Any workflow that calls RunAgentSession and inspects result.SessionID or result.StopReason will always receive empty strings, making downstream correlation and audit queries impossible without separate lookups. The documented contract ('Empty when the agent produced no session/update messages') is always true even when sessions did run.
Fix: After acpClient.Connect() returns, if acpClient.SessionCount() > 0, iterate over known session IDs (expose a SessionIDs() []string method on Client, or use SessionStats) and populate result.SessionID with the first session ID and result.StopReason from its EndReason.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
IMPORTANT-2: ActivityFunctions slice in workflow.go is stale/inconsistent with RegisterWorkflows.

What: ActivityFunctions (workflow.go lines 467-475) lists 7 activities but RegisterWorkflows (lines 440-455) registers 9 activities. RunAgentSession and QuerySessionEntries are registered in RegisterWorkflows but absent from ActivityFunctions.
Where: internal/temporal/workflow.go lines 467-475 vs lines 447-454
Why: ActivityFunctions is a publicly exported var that callers may use for registration. If any code path uses ActivityFunctions instead of RegisterWorkflows, it will miss RunAgentSession and QuerySessionEntries.
Impact: If pastured or a test harness uses ActivityFunctions for worker registration instead of RegisterWorkflows, RunAgentSession and QuerySessionEntries will silently not be available. This is a latent correctness bug waiting to surface.
Fix: Either (a) remove ActivityFunctions entirely since RegisterWorkflows is the canonical registration point and ActivityFunctions is not used anywhere currently, or (b) keep them in sync by deriving ActivityFunctions from the same list used by RegisterWorkflows.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
IMPORTANT-3: HandleSessionEnd cleanup-after-hook ordering leaves a window for duplicate HandleSessionEnd calls on the same session to double-count entriesWritten.

What: In HandleSessionEnd (session_handler.go lines 99-135), the session is deleted from h.updates at the very end (after hook dispatch). A concurrent or re-entrant HandleSessionEnd call for the same sessionID during hook dispatch will see a non-empty snapshot and re-process + re-persist all accumulated updates, incrementing entriesWritten twice.
Where: internal/acp/session_handler.go lines 99-134
Why: The snapshot is taken under lock (lines 100-105), but the delete happens in a separate lock acquisition at line 131. Between those two points, another goroutine calling HandleSessionEnd for the same session will also take a non-nil snapshot.
Impact: Duplicate entries in the audit trail for the same session. The acp.Client itself prevents this (processUpdate calls HandleSessionEnd only once per session per StopReason), but IndexingSessionHandler's contract says 'All methods are safe for concurrent invocation', so the implementation should be robust to double-end. 
Fix: Delete the session from h.updates immediately after taking the snapshot (under the same lock acquisition at lines 100-105), before any I/O or hook dispatch. This makes HandleSessionEnd idempotent for duplicate calls.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING-1 (BLOCKER): Dual audit Trail singleton — two independent singletons managing the same Trail.

What: internal/audit/activities.go defines InitTrail()/trail singleton with RecordAuditEvent, QueryAuditEvents, RecordSessionEntries, QuerySessionEntries. internal/temporal/activities.go defines a SEPARATE InitAuditTrail()/auditTrail singleton with identically-named functions wrapping the same Trail interface.

Where: internal/audit/activities.go:17-103 vs internal/temporal/activities.go:20-303

Why: Two singletons tracking the same resource is an API design defect. They can diverge: cmd/pastured/main.go calls only temporal.InitAuditTrail (line 144) but never audit.InitTrail. This means audit.RecordAuditEvent/QueryAuditEvents always fail with 'audit trail not initialized' if called. Additionally, the audit package exports Temporal-compatible activity functions that are never registered with the Temporal worker (RegisterWorkflows in workflow.go:447-454 registers temporal.RecordAuditEvent, not audit.RecordAuditEvent), making the audit package activities dead code.

Fix: Consolidate to one singleton location. Either:
(a) Keep audit.InitTrail as the canonical singleton; have temporal activities delegate to audit.RecordAuditEvent/etc. (audit is the owning package of Trail), or
(b) Remove activity wrappers from internal/audit/activities.go entirely, keeping InitTrail only for testing infra. The current dual-singleton design is confusing and the audit package activities are unreachable via the registered Temporal worker.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
MINOR-1: QuerySessionEntries orders by id ASC, not entry_index ASC. The doc says 'ascending entry_index order' but the query uses ORDER BY id ASC.

What: The QuerySessionEntries SQL query orders by the autoincrement id column, not by entry_index.
Where: internal/audit/sqlite.go line 324 ('ORDER BY id ASC')
Why: For correctly formed data inserted sequentially, id and entry_index will be monotonically consistent. However the interface doc says 'ascending entry_index for SQLite'. If batch inserts are not perfectly ordered by entry_index (e.g. retried writes with different entry_index values in the same batch), id order and entry_index order may diverge.
Impact: Minor risk of out-of-order playback in edge cases. The current insertion pattern (batch per session end, ordered by index from SharedIndexer) makes this a non-issue today.
Fix: Change 'ORDER BY id ASC' to 'ORDER BY entry_index ASC, id ASC' to match the documented contract. This also makes the schema's entry_index field queryable for range operations in the future.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
MINOR-2: TestHandleSessionEndEmptyUpdates asserts HookSessionEnded fires for a ghost session — but this is questionable semantics.

What: The test TestHandleSessionEndEmptyUpdates (session_handler_test.go lines 212-247) asserts that HookSessionEnded fires even when no updates were ever accumulated (a 'ghost' session). This means consumers see a session-ended event for a session they never saw a session-started event for.
Where: internal/acp/session_handler_test.go lines 236-246
Why: The current HandleSessionEnd dispatches HookSessionEnded unconditionally after the indexing/persistence step. For a ghost session, there are no entries, so the hook fires with entriesRecorded=0 and no preceding HookSessionStarted.
Impact: Hook consumers that maintain session state keyed by session ID will receive an unmatched end event, potentially triggering errors or stale cleanup.
Fix: Either (a) guard HookSessionEnded dispatch with 'if len(snapshot) > 0', or (b) update the test and documentation to explicitly document this as intentional 'best-effort cleanup' semantics. The current test appears to be asserting this as correct, but the comment says 'HookSessionEnded should still have fired (best-effort)' without explaining why that is desirable.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING-2 (IMPORTANT): RunAgentSessionResult.SessionID and StopReason fields are declared but never populated.

What: RunAgentSessionResult declares SessionID (string) and StopReason (string) fields with clear documentation. The RunAgentSession implementation builds the result with only EntriesRecorded set (line 349-351). There is a comment block (lines 353-361) that says 'We return the first session ID seen' but the code never calls acpClient.SessionStats() to retrieve it and never assigns result.SessionID or result.StopReason.

Where: internal/temporal/activities.go:246-364

Why: The result type advertises fields that are always empty strings. Callers relying on result.SessionID for correlation (audit lookups, observability) will silently get empty data. This is an interface-spec mismatch that will cause hard-to-debug gaps in session correlation at runtime.

Fix: After Connect returns, iterate sessions via acpClient.SessionCount()/acpClient.SessionStats(sessionID) (the Client already exposes SessionStats). Populate result.SessionID and result.StopReason from the first (or most recent) session's stats. The scaffolding comment already describes the intent — the implementation just needs to follow through.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
MINOR-3: Duplicate audit trail singleton pattern — both audit.InitTrail (audit/activities.go) and temporal.InitAuditTrail (temporal/activities.go) exist and maintain separate module-level Trail singletons for the same underlying concern.

What: There are two independent singleton patterns managing the same audit trail dependency: audit.InitTrail / audit.trail (in audit/activities.go) and temporal.InitAuditTrail / temporal.auditTrail (in temporal/activities.go). pastured only calls temporal.InitAuditTrail.
Where: internal/audit/activities.go lines 17-37 vs internal/temporal/activities.go lines 20-31
Why: The audit package defines its own activity wrappers (RecordAuditEvent, QueryAuditEvents, RecordSessionEntries, QuerySessionEntries) that would use audit.trail — but pastured registers the temporal.* equivalents, not the audit.* ones.
Impact: audit.InitTrail is never called in pastured, so if any code calls the audit.* activity wrappers directly (instead of temporal.*), it will receive 'audit trail not initialized' errors. This is confusing for future contributors.
Fix: Either (a) remove audit/activities.go and consolidate all activity wrappers in temporal/activities.go, or (b) have temporal.InitAuditTrail also call audit.InitTrail, or (c) document explicitly why both exist and which is canonical.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING-3 (IMPORTANT): hooks.InitHooksManager called AFTER temporal.RegisterWorkflows — race window exists.

What: In cmd/pastured/main.go, the sequence is: (6) worker.New + temporal.RegisterWorkflows (line 173-174), then (7) hooks.InitHooksManager (lines 182-183), then (8) w.Run(worker.InterruptCh()) in a goroutine (line 195). The worker is not started before step 8, so there is no actual race at runtime in normal operation. However, the comment on step 7 says 'Initialise hooks Manager' but the worker is started immediately afterward via goroutine. If any workflow or activity tasks arrive between worker.New and the goroutine start, hooksMgr will still be nil and hooks dispatches will silently no-op instead of firing handlers. More importantly, the ordering is semantically wrong: hooks should be initialized BEFORE the worker is registered, not after, to avoid any ambiguity about initialization order guarantees.

Where: cmd/pastured/main.go:172-196

Why: Design-by-convention: the documented startup sequence in run() comments says steps 1-8 in order. Having hooksMgr initialized after RegisterWorkflows but before w.Run means the window is safe only because w.Run is goroutine-launched, but this is fragile and relies on goroutine scheduling. It will confuse future maintainers adding real hook handlers.

Fix: Move hooks.InitHooksManager (step 7) before temporal.RegisterWorkflows (step 6), or at minimum before worker.New. This matches the pattern already used for audit trail (initialized before RegisterWorkflows at step 3).

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING-4 (MINOR): sliceWorkflowFn unexported alias is dead code.

What: internal/temporal/workflow_slice.go line 243 declares 'var sliceWorkflowFn = SliceWorkflowFn' with comment 'alias for RegisterWorkflows backward compatibility'. RegisterWorkflows uses SliceWorkflowFn directly (workflow.go:445). The alias is never referenced anywhere in the codebase.

Where: internal/temporal/workflow_slice.go:242-243

Why: Dead code and misleading comment ('backward compatibility' implies history that does not exist in this codebase). Creates confusion for readers trying to understand the registration path.

Fix: Remove the var sliceWorkflowFn alias entirely.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

Reviewed all three slices (SLICE-1 audit trail, SLICE-2 hooks singleton, SLICE-3 SessionHandler + pastured wiring) against the Correctness axis.

Summary:
- No BLOCKERs found. All production code paths are reachable and logically correct.
- Tests pass (audit, hooks, acp packages all green).
- Build is clean (CGO_ENABLED=0 go build ./... produces no errors).

3 IMPORTANT findings (tracked in aura-plugins-pexyy):
1. RunAgentSessionResult.SessionID/StopReason always empty — result fields declared but never populated.
2. ActivityFunctions slice missing RunAgentSession and QuerySessionEntries — latent registration bug.
3. HandleSessionEnd has a double-end race window — concurrent calls on same sessionID would double-persist.

3 MINOR findings (tracked in aura-plugins-srav7):
1. QuerySessionEntries orders by id ASC, not entry_index ASC as documented.
2. HookSessionEnded fires for ghost sessions (no preceding HookSessionStarted) — questionable semantics.
3. Dual audit trail singleton pattern (audit.InitTrail vs temporal.InitAuditTrail) — confusing for contributors.

None of the IMPORTANT items break the happy path for a single-agent session. The race in IMPORTANT-3 requires two concurrent HandleSessionEnd calls on the same sessionID, which the acp.Client prevents in normal usage. IMPORTANT-1 and IMPORTANT-2 are functional gaps that should be addressed in the follow-up epic.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING-5 (MINOR): ActivityFunctions slice is declared but never used.

What: internal/temporal/workflow.go:467-475 declares var ActivityFunctions = []interface{}{...} listing 7 activity functions. No caller in cmd/pastured/main.go or any test file references this variable. The worker registration is done exclusively through RegisterWorkflows(w) which calls r.RegisterActivity() directly (lines 447-454). ActivityFunctions is inconsistent with RegisterWorkflows (missing RunAgentSession, hooks.DispatchHook).

Where: internal/temporal/workflow.go:467-475

Why: Misleading exported API — consumers might attempt to use ActivityFunctions for worker registration and get an incomplete list (missing RunAgentSession and hooks.DispatchHook). Risk of divergence as more activities are added.

Fix: Either remove ActivityFunctions entirely (RegisterWorkflows is the canonical registration path), or update it to match RegisterWorkflows exactly and document the intended use case (e.g., testing).

author: David Huu Pham
text:
## Reviewer B (Test Quality) — Round 1 Review

### IMPORTANT — F1: RunAgentSession activity has zero test coverage

What: temporal.RunAgentSession (activities.go:320) is registered as a Temporal activity and wired into pastured, but there are no tests for it — not in temporal_test.go, not anywhere else.
Where: internal/temporal/activities.go:320, internal/temporal/temporal_test.go (absent)
Why: This is the primary new I/O boundary introduced in SLICE-3. The uninitialized-trail path (returns non-retryable ApplicationError), the Connect-error path, and the result-summary path are all untested. If the trail guard or connect-error handling regresses, it will only surface in production.
Fix: Add at minimum two activity-environment tests:
  1. TestRunAgentSession_UninitializedTrail — call with nil auditTrail, assert non-retryable ApplicationError.
  2. TestRunAgentSession_ConnectError — inject a stubbed ACP client (or pass a bogus agentCmd), assert that a wrapped error is returned (not nil).
These can use the Temporal testsuite.WorkflowTestSuite + NewTestActivityEnvironment() pattern already used in this file.

### IMPORTANT — F2: temporal.RecordSessionEntries and QuerySessionEntries activity wrappers have no tests

What: Two new Temporal activity wrappers, temporal.RecordSessionEntries (activities.go:264) and temporal.QuerySessionEntries (activities.go:287), are registered in the worker but have no tests. The audit.RecordSessionEntries / QuerySessionEntries wrappers in the audit package DO have test equivalents, but the temporal-layer wrappers with their own nil-trail guard and error-wrapping are untested.
Where: internal/temporal/activities.go:264,287; internal/temporal/temporal_test.go (absent)
Why: The nil-trail guard and error-formatting differ slightly between the audit layer and the temporal layer. The temporal layer uses temporal.NewNonRetryableApplicationError while the audit layer uses fmt.Errorf. A regression in the guard type would only appear in production Temporal retry behaviour.
Fix: Add TestRecordSessionEntries_UninitializedTrail and TestQuerySessionEntries_UninitializedTrail in temporal_test.go, mirroring the existing TestRecordAuditEvent_UninitializedTrail pattern (line 1002).

### MINOR — F3: audit.RecordSessionEntries and QuerySessionEntries activity wrappers lack roundtrip tests

What: activities_test.go has TestActivityRoundtrip_WithInMemory for RecordAuditEvent/QueryAuditEvents, but no equivalent roundtrip test for the new RecordSessionEntries and QuerySessionEntries activity wrappers.
Where: internal/audit/activities_test.go (absent)
Why: The errorTrail stub satisfies the Trail interface but the actual activity wrapper behaviour (nil guard, error propagation) is only confirmed through interface satisfaction, not execution.
Fix: Add two tests mirroring TestActivityRoundtrip_WithInMemory:
  - TestSessionEntriesActivityRoundtrip_WithInMemory
  - TestActivityRoundtrip_SessionEntriesPropagatesTrailError

### MINOR — F4: IndexingSessionHandler.EntriesRecorded() is not tested

What: The public EntriesRecorded() method on IndexingSessionHandler (session_handler.go:141) is never called in session_handler_test.go.
Where: internal/acp/session_handler_test.go (absent), internal/acp/session_handler.go:141
Why: This is a public method explicitly documented as safe for concurrent use. Its counter is updated under a mutex (lines 118-121). Neither the counter accuracy nor its concurrent safety is verified.
Fix: Extend TestHandleSessionEndIndexesAndPersists or add a dedicated test asserting that EntriesRecorded() returns the expected count after HandleSessionEnd.

### MINOR — F5: No concurrent test for IndexingSessionHandler multiple parallel sessions

What: session_handler_test.go tests multiple sessions sequentially but never tests concurrent HandleUpdate calls from multiple goroutines for different sessions simultaneously.
Where: internal/acp/session_handler_test.go (absent)
Why: The handler uses sync.Mutex to protect the updates map. While correct by inspection, concurrent access under race detector pressure would expose any missed locking. The InMemoryAuditTrail and SqliteAuditTrail both have explicit concurrent tests — the IndexingSessionHandler does not.
Fix: Add TestHandleUpdate_ConcurrentSessions using goroutines and sync.WaitGroup, running with -race in CI (the Makefile already has test-race target).

### MINOR — F6: SQLite batch insert large-row path not tested

What: TestSqliteAuditTrail_SessionEntrySuite exercises 2-5 entries per call. RecordSessionEntries in sqlite.go uses a transactional prepared statement that is shared across all rows. A large batch (e.g. 100+ entries) is never tested.
Where: internal/audit/sqlite_test.go
Why: The prepared-statement-in-transaction path has a deferred rollback on named return err. A single INSERT failure mid-batch should roll back all rows. This is not tested; a partial-commit bug would produce data loss silently.
Fix: Add TestSqliteAuditTrail_SessionEntries_LargeBatch inserting 100 entries in one call, then verifying all 100 are returned by QuerySessionEntries.

### POSITIVE observations

- Shared runSessionEntrySuite fixture in audit_test.go is excellent — single source of truth for both InMemoryAuditTrail and SqliteAuditTrail correctness.
- singleton_test.go correctly uses t.Cleanup(func() { hooks.InitHooksManager(nil) }) on every test that mutates global state, and never calls t.Parallel on singleton-mutating tests.
- captureHandler in session_handler_test.go directly injects a Manager (not the singleton), correctly avoiding global state dependency.
- nil hooksMgr path is explicitly tested (TestNilHooksMgrIsNoOp) — good edge-case coverage.
- Durability tests for both audit events and session entries in sqlite_test.go cover the close/reopen cycle cleanly.
- TestDispatchHook_ContextCancelled_NilManager_IsNoOp catches the nil-manager + cancelled context path.
- Hook dispatch error propagation is tested in TestDispatchHook_WithManager_HandlerError_Propagated.

author: David Huu Pham
text: F1: RunAgentSession activity has zero test coverage. Temporal activity registered in pastured but nil-trail guard, Connect-error path, and result summary are all untested. See aura-plugins-3bwnx for details.

author: David Huu Pham
text: F2: temporal.RecordSessionEntries and QuerySessionEntries wrappers have no tests. These use temporal.NewNonRetryableApplicationError (not fmt.Errorf) for nil-trail guard — that difference is untested. See aura-plugins-3bwnx for details.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING-6 (MINOR): EpochWorkflow records phase transition audit event twice per transition.

What: In workflow.go EpochWorkflow.Run(), on every advance, two separate audit activities are called back-to-back:
- RecordTransition (line 206) — writes a full TransitionRecord to the audit trail as an AuditEvent
- RecordAuditEvent (line 223) — writes ANOTHER AuditEvent with EventType=EventPhaseTransition for the same transition

Both encode the from/to phase transition for the same epoch transition event. The result is that every transition generates two audit rows for what is logically one event.

Where: internal/temporal/workflow.go:204-227

Why: Duplicated writes are not catastrophic but they bloat the audit log and will confuse query consumers (QueryAuditEvents will return 2 rows per transition). RecordTransition already calls auditTrail.RecordEvent internally, so the subsequent explicit RecordAuditEvent call is redundant.

Fix: Remove the explicit RecordAuditEvent call in EpochWorkflow.Run (lines 211-227), or clearly distinguish the two by using different EventTypes (e.g. EventTransitionAttempt vs EventPhaseTransition) and document the intent. If both are needed for different consumers, add a comment explaining why.

author: David Huu Pham
text: F3: audit.RecordSessionEntries/QuerySessionEntries activity wrappers lack roundtrip tests in activities_test.go.

author: David Huu Pham
text: F4: IndexingSessionHandler.EntriesRecorded() public method is never called in tests.

author: David Huu Pham
text: F5: No concurrent test for IndexingSessionHandler with multiple parallel sessions (go test -race).

author: David Huu Pham
text: F6: SQLite RecordSessionEntries large-batch path (100+ rows) and mid-batch rollback are not tested.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE — Two IMPORTANT gaps in test coverage:
1. temporal.RunAgentSession is a public Temporal activity with zero tests (nil-trail guard, connect-error path untested).
2. temporal.RecordSessionEntries and QuerySessionEntries wrappers also have no tests.
Both are registered in the worker and live on the critical data path. Fix: add activity-environment tests for uninitialized-trail and error-propagation paths following the existing TestRecordAuditEvent_UninitializedTrail pattern. Four MINOR gaps also documented above.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE (Reviewer C — Elegance axis)

Review task: aura-plugins-h5pii
Severity groups:
  BLOCKER: aura-plugins-4jsgx
  IMPORTANT: aura-plugins-rd0oy
  MINOR: aura-plugins-gnkk5

Summary of findings:

BLOCKER (1):
  F1 — Dual audit Trail singleton: internal/audit/activities.go and internal/temporal/activities.go each maintain their own independent singleton wrapping the same audit.Trail interface. cmd/pastured/main.go only calls temporal.InitAuditTrail, leaving audit.InitTrail uncalled — so all audit package activity wrappers will always fail. The audit package activities are also never registered with the Temporal worker (RegisterWorkflows registers temporal.* variants), making them dead code. Consolidate to one canonical singleton.

IMPORTANT (2):
  F2 — RunAgentSessionResult.SessionID and StopReason are declared but never populated. The code has a comment saying it 'returns the first session ID seen' but never calls acpClient.SessionStats(). Callers get empty strings for these fields.
  F3 — hooks.InitHooksManager is called after temporal.RegisterWorkflows (main.go step 7 follows step 6). Should match the audit trail pattern: initialize hooks before registering with the worker to avoid any ordering ambiguity.

MINOR (3):
  F4 — sliceWorkflowFn alias is dead code.
  F5 — ActivityFunctions exported slice is unused and is inconsistent with RegisterWorkflows (missing 2 activities).
  F6 — EpochWorkflow records each phase transition twice: once via RecordTransition (which calls auditTrail.RecordEvent internally) and again via an explicit RecordAuditEvent call with the same EventType.

author: David Huu Pham
text: Review cycle 1 fixes complete: FIX-1 (BLOCKER) removed dead audit activity wrappers from internal/audit/activities.go — canonical activities remain in internal/temporal/activities.go only; FIX-2 added LastSessionID()/LastStopReason() to IndexingSessionHandler and populated RunAgentSessionResult fields; FIX-3 added RunAgentSession to ActivityFunctions slice; FIX-4 moved delete(h.updates, sessionID) inside initial lock acquisition to close double-end race; FIX-5 added TestRunAgentSession_UninitializedTrail and TestRunAgentSession_ConnectError; FIX-6 added TestRecordSessionEntries_UninitializedTrail and TestQuerySessionEntries_UninitializedTrail; FIX-7 moved hooks.InitHooksManager before temporal.RegisterWorkflows. All tests pass (go test ./...).

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING IMPORTANT-1: TestRunAgentSession_ConnectError does not assert error type or retryability

What: The test only checks `err != nil`. It does not verify whether the connect error is returned as a Temporal non-retryable ApplicationError vs a plain retryable error.

Where: internal/temporal/temporal_test.go:TestRunAgentSession_ConnectError (line 1130) + internal/temporal/activities.go:RunAgentSession line 339-345.

Why: In RunAgentSession, the AuditTrailUninitialized check returns a NonRetryableApplicationError (line 328), but the acpClient.Connect failure wraps the error with plain fmt.Errorf (line 340). This means a missing binary will be retried by Temporal indefinitely. The test passes because err != nil, but it does not catch this semantics gap.

Impact on end-user: An operator who provides a wrong agent binary path will watch Temporal retry the activity until it times out rather than receiving an immediate non-retryable failure. This wastes Temporal activity slots and obscures the root cause.

Fix: The test should assert that the error wraps a StructuredError with Category=CategoryConnection. Better: RunAgentSession should wrap the connect error in a NewNonRetryableApplicationError for CategoryConnection errors (binary not found is not a transient condition). The test should then assert temporal.IsNonRetryableApplicationError(err).

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING IMPORTANT-2: TestHandleSessionEndEmptyUpdates asserts HookSessionEnded fires for ghost sessions, but this is untested production behavior

What: The test at session_handler_test.go line 238 asserts that HookSessionEnded fires with 'ended=1' even when no updates were ever accumulated for the session.

Where: internal/acp/session_handler_test.go:TestHandleSessionEndEmptyUpdates (line 214). Production code: session_handler.go HandleSessionEnd unconditionally calls dispatchHook at line 133.

Why: The test comment says 'HookSessionEnded should still have fired (best-effort)' — this is intentional design. However, the captureHandler only registers for HookSessionStarted and HookSessionEnded. The ghost session never produced a HookSessionStarted event, so a consumer that correlates started/ended pairs will see an unmatched end event. The test does not assert that HookSessionStarted was NOT fired for the ghost session (it only checks ended=1), leaving the asymmetry undocumented and uncheckable.

Impact on end-user: Any hook consumer (e.g. dashboard, cost tracker) that expects start-before-end pairing will see a dangling end event and may miscalculate session counts.

Fix: Add an assertion in TestHandleSessionEndEmptyUpdates that cap.received contains 0 HookSessionStarted events for the ghost session, making the documented asymmetry explicit and tested.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING MINOR-1: audit/activities_test.go tests only InitTrail singleton behavior, not the activity wrappers in that package

What: internal/audit/activities_test.go contains only TestInitTrail_NilResetsState and TestInitTrail_ReplacesImpl. The audit package's activities.go only exports InitTrail (no other activities in that file). This is correct — the real activity wrappers (RecordAuditEvent, QueryAuditEvents, etc.) live in internal/temporal/activities.go and are tested in temporal_test.go. However, the file name 'activities_test.go' is misleading: a reader expecting activity coverage will look here and find only singleton lifecycle tests.

Where: internal/audit/activities_test.go

Why: The tests are correct and meaningful for what they do. The naming causes confusion about scope.

Fix: Rename internal/audit/activities_test.go to internal/audit/singleton_test.go (or trail_singleton_test.go) to make its scope clear. This is cosmetic and does not affect coverage.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
FINDING MINOR-2: TestRunAgentSession_UninitializedTrail does not assert non-retryability

What: The test checks err != nil but not that the error is a Temporal non-retryable ApplicationError.

Where: internal/temporal/temporal_test.go:TestRunAgentSession_UninitializedTrail (line 1108). Compare with TestRecordAuditEvent_UninitializedTrail which has the same gap.

Why: The production code correctly returns temporal.NewNonRetryableApplicationError (line 328 of activities.go). The test does not validate this semantic. Every other uninitialized-trail test has the same gap — they all say 'expected error ... got nil' but never validate the error type.

Fix: Add temporal.IsNonRetryableApplicationError(err) assertion to each uninitialized-trail test (TestRunAgentSession_UninitializedTrail, TestRecordSessionEntries_UninitializedTrail, TestQuerySessionEntries_UninitializedTrail, TestRecordAuditEvent_UninitializedTrail, TestQueryAuditEvents_UninitializedTrail). This ensures the non-retryable contract is tested, not just that an error is returned.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT — Reviewer A (Correctness), Round 2.

All 7 Round-1 findings verified fixed and all tests pass (go test + go vet clean).

FIX-1 (BLOCKER — Dead audit singleton): audit/activities.go now contains only InitTrail + the singleton var. All 11 activity wrappers that duplicated temporal/activities.go are gone. audit/activities_test.go rewritten to test only InitTrail behaviour (nil reset and replacement). No production code calls audit.InitTrail outside of tests.

FIX-2 (IMPORTANT — RunAgentSessionResult not populated): lastSessionID and lastStopReason fields added to IndexingSessionHandler. Both written under the same mu.Lock in HandleSessionEnd (after indexing/persistence). LastSessionID()/LastStopReason() accessors added with proper locking. RunAgentSession reads them after acpClient.Connect() returns and sets result.SessionID and result.StopReason. Correct.

FIX-3 (IMPORTANT — ActivityFunctions slice): RunAgentSession and hooks.DispatchHook now appear in ActivityFunctions (lines 468–477 of workflow.go). Slice matches RegisterWorkflows exactly. Verified by inspection.

FIX-4 (IMPORTANT — HandleSessionEnd double-end race): delete(h.updates, sessionID) moved into the initial snapshot lock block (before Unlock). A second concurrent HandleSessionEnd for the same sessionID will take an empty snapshot (zero-copy) and write zero entries — correct, idempotent, no double-write. The subsequent lock for counters/lastSessionID/lastStopReason is a separate, independent acquisition — no lock inversion, no deadlock risk. Only h.mu is ever acquired in this function; there is no nested locking. Analysis: safe.

FIX-5 (IMPORTANT — TestRunAgentSession tests): TestRunAgentSession_UninitializedTrail and TestRunAgentSession_ConnectError added in temporal_test.go (lines 1108–1150). Both run as activity harness tests using Temporal testsuite. Uninitialized trail test verifies non-retryable error. Connect error test uses /no-such-binary-pasture-test-xyz and verifies error returned. Both pass.

FIX-6 (IMPORTANT — temporal session entry wrapper tests): TestRecordSessionEntries_UninitializedTrail and TestQuerySessionEntries_UninitializedTrail added (lines 1155–1189). Same pattern as other uninitialized-trail tests. Both pass.

FIX-7 (IMPORTANT — hooks init ordering): hooks.InitHooksManager now at line 178, worker.New at line 182, temporal.RegisterWorkflows at line 183. Correct order: audit trail → Temporal client → search attributes → hooks → worker + workflows. Activities dispatched during workflow execution will find an initialized hooks manager.

No regressions introduced. go vet clean. All 43+ temporal tests, 18+ acp tests, 8+ audit tests, 12 pastured cmd tests pass.

author: David Huu Pham

author: David Huu Pham
text:
UAT revisions complete:

Rev 1 (flush every update): IndexingSessionHandler now persists entries on EVERY HandleUpdate call instead of accumulating until session end. Replaced updates map[string][]SessionUpdate with sessions map[string]bool for session-start dedup. EntriesRecorded counter increments per flush. Tests added: TestHandleUpdatePersistsImmediately, TestEntriesRecordedCountsAcrossUpdates. TestMultipleSessionsAreIndependent updated to verify per-update flush semantics.

Rev 2 (structured hook warnings): All hook dispatch error sites use slog.Warn with structured fields (what/why/impact/fix/event/epochId). Applied in: Activities.CheckConstraints, Activities.RecordTransition, Activities.DispatchHook (activities.go), and IndexingSessionHandler.dispatchHook (session_handler.go). No errors silently swallowed.

Rev 3 (Activity struct DI): Replaced singleton pattern with Temporal activity struct. Created Activities{Trail, HooksMgr} struct in internal/temporal/activities.go. Converted all free activity functions to methods. Removed auditTrail singleton + InitAuditTrail() from activities.go. Removed hooksMgr singleton + InitHooksManager()/GetManager()/DispatchHook() from hooks/singleton.go. Moved DispatchHook as Activities method. RegisterWorkflows now accepts *Activities; registers via r.RegisterActivity(acts). EpochWorkflow activity calls use string name constants (ActivityCheckConstraints, etc.). cmd/pastured/main.go constructs Activities{Trail, HooksMgr} and passes to RegisterWorkflows. temporal_test.go updated to use DI pattern (newActivities helper + env.RegisterActivity(acts)).

All 12 packages build + test pass.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
## Phase 4 Review Round 1 — Reviewer C (Elegance) REVISE

### Finding 1 [CRITICAL, confidence 95%]: templ is wrong for non-HTML output
templ is an HTML component library whose HTML escaping will corrupt Markdown and XML output. text/template with missingkey=error is the correct Go replacement for Jinja2 with StrictUndefined parity.

### Finding 2 [IMPORTANT, confidence 88%]: cmd/pasture-codegen should be //go:generate
Go idiom for dev-only codegen tools is `//go:generate go run ./tools/codegen` not a standalone binary. This keeps codegen out of the binary distribution.

### Finding 3 [IMPORTANT, confidence 85%]: internal/codegen package decomposition needs explicit file boundaries
The proposal lists files but doesn't specify which types/functions live where. Need explicit interface contracts.

### Reviewers A and B: Session compacted before results returned. Treating as incomplete — will address Reviewer C findings and re-review with all 3.

author: David Huu Pham
text: SUPERSEDED by PROPOSAL-2 (aura-plugins-onfwy): templ→text/template, cmd/pasture-codegen→//go:generate, explicit package decomposition.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

Reviewer B — Test Quality axis
Round: 1
Confidence: High (90%)

## Summary

The testing strategy in PROPOSAL-2 is well-aligned with the user's stated principle: test public behaviour and observable contracts, not internal implementation details. No blocking issues found.

## Criterion-by-Criterion Assessment

### 1. YAML fixtures via testutil.LoadFixtures — PASS

AC7 explicitly requires all tests use YAML fixtures via testutil.LoadFixtures. The proposal calls for testdata/ directories under each *_test.go file's package, which is exactly the pattern in internal/acp/content_block_test.go (YAML fixture loaded, struct unmarshalled, table-driven subtests). The fixture constants (ContentBlock, CLISmoke, RunAgentSession, ConfigLoading) will need new entries — one gap: the proposal does not state that new FixtureName constants (e.g. CodegenMarkers, CodegenSkills, CodegenSchema) will be added to testutil/fixtures.go. This is minor; the existing pattern makes it obvious, but the proposal should confirm this is required. Not a blocker.

### 2. Test boundaries (public API, not internals) — PASS

The proposed public surfaces — FindMarkerPositions, ReplaceMarkerRegion, GenerateSchema, GenerateSkill, GenerateSubSkill, GetRoleContext, GetPhaseContext — are all functions the caller actually uses. The context_test.go tests verify constraint IDs per role/phase, which is a public contract (what constraints does this role own?), not an internal data structure. This matches the Python pattern in test_gen_skills.py (TestHeaderUpdatedBodyPreserved, TestMarkerErrors) which tests public generate_skill() not private _render_header(). Good alignment.

One note: markers.go lists FindMarkerPositions as a public function. The Python equivalent _find_marker_positions is private. Exposing it in Go is acceptable — it makes the function independently testable — but the proposal should not test it via reflection into template execution internals.

### 3. Error path coverage — PASS WITH OBSERVATION

The proposal explicitly names four MarkerError cases: missing markers, reversed markers, duplicate markers, missing-only-one-side. This matches the Python _find_marker_positions error taxonomy exactly. The schema_test.go will cover GenerateSchema(w io.Writer) error path via a bad writer. One gap not mentioned: what happens when a template file is missing from the embedded FS (templatesFS)? text/template.ParseFS returns an error; the proposal does not call out this error path in the testing strategy. This is important to cover in implementation (skills_test.go) but not a blocker at proposal stage.

### 4. 'Contains expected sections' granularity — CORRECT

The user explicitly said 'We shouldn't be testing exact CLI stdout outputs, because those are prone to change and are brittle.' The proposal's 'generate → verify contains expected sections' strategy for skill and agent tests is exactly right. The Python tests (test_gen_skills.py TestHeaderUpdatedBodyPreserved.test_generated_header_contains_role_name) do the same: assert RoleId.Worker.value in result. This is the correct granularity: stable semantic assertions (role name present, constraints section present, markers present) rather than full-string equality.

### 5. Round-trip testing for schema.xml — PASS

The proposal says 'Round-trip XML validation tests (YAML fixtures)' for schema_test.go. The Python equivalent generates schema.xml into a temp file, then parses it back with schema_parser.parse_schema and does field-by-field matching (test_gen_schema.py TestRoundTripConsistency). The Go version should do analogously: GenerateSchema(w) → encoding/xml Unmarshal back to structs → check field counts/IDs match ROLE_SPECS/CONSTRAINT_SPECS etc. The proposal does not define a Go schema parser, but encoding/xml can unmarshal the output directly without one. Round-trip is sufficient — no need for a full validator in MVP.

### 6. Integration test (go generate produces no diff) — GOOD

AC6 + the go:generate no-diff integration test is the right capstone. It catches template regressions, data drift, and wiring bugs in one shot. This aligns with 'integration tests are of the most important value.'

## Observations (non-blocking)

- The proposal does not specify that new FixtureName constants will be added to internal/testutil/fixtures.go. Workers should add them (e.g. CodegenMarkers, CodegenContext, CodegenSchema, CodegenSkills, CodegenAgents) to keep the typed-fixture pattern consistent.
- The missing-template-from-FS error path (ParseFS failure) should be covered in skills_test.go / agents_test.go as an error case.
- Context tests should verify not just that the correct constraint IDs are present but also that no unexpected IDs appear (subset vs exact set). The Python test pattern uses set equality, not subset. Workers should mirror this.

## Vote

ACCEPT — The testing strategy is sound, uses the right abstractions, covers the critical error paths, and matches the existing codebase patterns. The observations above are implementation guidance for workers, not blockers for the proposal.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

## CODEGEN-REVIEW-A-1: Correctness axis

**Reviewer A** — Correctness (spirit and technicality)

---

### Summary

The proposal correctly mirrors the Python codegen pipeline. The architecture accurately traces the data flow from canonical spec dicts through generators to output files. I have one notable concern (confidence: medium) about a missing spec type, and several lower-confidence observations, none of which rise to REVISE level on their own. Overall the proposal is sound for a plan review.

---

### Finding 1: FigureSpec / figure loading omitted from spec types (MODERATE concern, confidence: high)

**What:** The Python pipeline has a significant Figure/FigureSpec subsystem:
- `FIGURE_SPECS: dict[FigureId, Figure]` in types.py
- `_load_figure_content(figure_id, figures_dir)` in context_injection.py — loads YAML at codegen time
- `_figures_for_command(command_id)` in gen_skills.py — figures keyed by `command_refs`
- `_figures_by_workflow(figures)` in gen_skills.py — figures keyed by `workflow_refs`
- `FIGURE_SPECS` used in gen_schema.py `_build_figures()`
- Sub-skill generation (`generate_sub_skill`) entirely depends on figures — if figures are missing, sub-skills silently produce empty/truncated output

**Where:** Proposal specs.go defines ConstraintSpec, RoleSpec, BehaviorSpec, CommandSpec, PhaseSpec, Transition, HandoffSpec. **FigureSpec is absent.** The proposal mentions `GenerateSubSkill(commandId, path, opts)` as a public function, and the template `skill_sub_figure.go.tmpl`, but the spec type that feeds it (FigureSpec with fields: id, title, type, role_refs, section_ref, workflow_refs, command_refs, content) is not defined in the proposal's type inventory.

**Impact:** Without FigureSpec in specs.go and corresponding data in specs_data.go, the sub-skill template has nothing to render. The acceptance criteria (AC5: GenerateAgent produces complete agent .md with constraints, behaviors, checklists) also does not mention figures, which appear in role-level SKILL.md headers via `figures_by_workflow`.

**Verdict:** This is a gap in the spec definition, not a fatal correctness error — the architecture diagram shows the correct data flow, and the implementer will need to add FigureSpec. The proposal is still implementable: the architect likely considered this an implementation detail since it mirrors the existing Python Figure dataclass exactly. However, the proposal's public interface table should call this out explicitly so S4 does not discover it mid-slice.

**Recommendation:** Add FigureSpec to the spec types inventory in specs.go, and add FigureId/FigureType/SectionRef enums where needed (or note they use string values to avoid a large enum list).

---

### Finding 2: generate_skill prefix-dropping vs generate_sub_skill prefix-preserving — asymmetry is correct (confidence: high)

The Python `generate_skill` (role-level) intentionally drops everything before the BEGIN marker (the template owns frontmatter + heading). The Python `generate_sub_skill` (command-level) preserves the prefix (the h1 heading before BEGIN is hand-authored). The proposal's `GenerateSkill` / `GenerateSubSkill` naming mirrors this split, and `ReplaceMarkerRegion` in markers.go needs to support both modes. This asymmetry must be explicit in markers.go — the proposal states `ReplaceMarkerRegion(content, rendered) string` but does not note whether it drops prefix or not. Implementer needs to handle both cases. Minor wording gap; does not block.

---

### Finding 3: text/template missingkey=error is correct parity for Jinja2 StrictUndefined (confidence: high)

`template.New("").Option("missingkey=error")` causes a runtime error when a template references a key not present in the data map — exactly matching Jinja2 `StrictUndefined` behavior. The Python code uses `env = Environment(undefined=StrictUndefined)`. The Go replacement is technically correct. The one difference is that Go templates use `{{.FieldName}}` (struct field or map key) while Jinja2 uses `{{ variable }}` — the template syntax rewrite is a translation concern for the implementer, not a correctness issue for the plan.

---

### Finding 4: encoding/xml vs Python xml.etree.ElementTree — correctness (confidence: high)

Python uses `xml.etree.ElementTree` with a custom `_indent()` helper and `html.unescape()` post-processing pass (to undo ET's escaping of text content that was already manually escaped). The Go proposal uses `encoding/xml` marshalling. Two sub-issues:

1. **Indentation:** Python applies a custom recursive `_indent()` pass. `encoding/xml` in Go does not pretty-print by default; the implementer must use `xml.MarshalIndent` or build the tree manually. The proposal does not specify which approach is used — this is an implementation detail to be resolved in S5.
2. **HTML entity round-trip:** The Python code calls `html.unescape()` after serialization to undo double-escaping of text that already contains XML entities. Go's `encoding/xml` escapes `<`, `>`, `&`, `"` in text nodes — the implementer must ensure the generated XML does not double-encode where constraint text contains angle brackets (e.g., in code examples with `<` comparisons). This is a known risk but addressable in S5.

Neither is a plan-level correctness blocker; both are implementation-time risks worth noting in S5's acceptance criteria.

---

### Finding 5: go:generate placement and tools/codegen path (confidence: medium)

The proposal places the `//go:generate go run ../../tools/codegen` directive in `internal/codegen/codegen.go`. The relative path `../../tools/codegen` resolves from `internal/codegen/` to `tools/codegen/` — this is correct. Running `go generate ./internal/codegen/...` from the module root will work. AC6 is achievable as stated.

---

### Finding 6: Slice wave decomposition — correctness (confidence: high)

Wave dependencies are correctly ordered:
- S1 (spec types + data) and S2 (context builders) and S3 (markers) are Wave 1 — all are leaf foundations.
- S4 (skills), S5 (schema), S6 (agents) are Wave 2 — they all depend on Wave 1 types.
- S7 (wiring) is Wave 3 — depends on all generators.

S2 depends on S1 (context builders use spec types). S3 is independent of S1 (marker surgery is pure string manipulation). The proposal does not call out the S2-depends-on-S1 dependency explicitly, but it is implied by the architecture. This is fine for planning purposes.

---

### Acceptance Criteria Completeness

AC1-AC7 are well-formed and verifiable. Two gaps:
- **AC3** (GenerateSchema produces valid XML) should note that the generated XML must be parseable by the existing `validate_schema.py` (or a Go equivalent) to be truly verifiable.
- **AC5** (GenerateAgent produces complete agent .md) should mention figure sections, since the Python agent template includes figures (workflow figures) via `figures_by_workflow`.

Neither is a plan blocker — they are clarifications for the implementer.

---

### Verdict

ACCEPT — The Go architecture correctly mirrors the Python pipeline. The text/template + go:generate approach is technically sound. The main gap (FigureSpec missing from spec type inventory) is a completeness issue in the proposal's documentation, not a fundamental architectural flaw — the architecture diagram correctly includes context.go as the figure-loading layer. The implementer will need to add FigureSpec to S1, but the proposal provides enough structure to proceed without a revision round.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
## PROPOSAL-2-REVIEW-C-1 — Reviewer C (Elegance) — VOTE: ACCEPT

### PROPOSAL-1 Findings Resolution

**Finding 1 [CRITICAL/95]: templ → text/template**
RESOLVED. text/template with Option("missingkey=error") correctly replaces templ. Jinja2 StrictUndefined parity is achieved. The Jinja2 templates' constructs (join, loop.last, lower, phase_slug dict lookup) all have direct FuncMap equivalents in the proposal.

**Finding 2 [IMPORTANT/88]: cmd/pasture-codegen → //go:generate**
RESOLVED. tools/codegen/main.go invoked via //go:generate directive is idiomatic Go. Keeps codegen out of binary distribution.

**Finding 3 [IMPORTANT/85]: explicit package decomposition**
RESOLVED. PROPOSAL-2 now specifies every file with its public function signatures (FindMarkerPositions, ReplaceMarkerRegion, GenerateSchema, GenerateSkill, GenerateSubSkill, GenerateAgent) and clear type ownership per file.

---

### New Findings

**[IMPORTANT, confidence 82]: Whitespace control gap — trim_blocks/lstrip_blocks have no Go equivalent**

The Python Jinja2 environment uses trim_blocks=True and lstrip_blocks=True on all three generators (gen_skills.py:287-288, gen_agents.py:91-92). These settings strip the newline after block tags ({% if %}, {% for %}, {% endfor %}) and leading whitespace before block tags. Go text/template has no equivalent settings — the template author must use {{- and -}} trim markers on every control flow tag, or restructure template whitespace manually.

The existing .j2 templates have control-flow tags on their own lines (e.g. {% if owned_phases %}, {% for phase in phases_detail %}) which produce clean output only because of trim_blocks/lstrip_blocks. Naively transliterating these to Go .go.tmpl files without trim markers will produce extra blank lines and leading whitespace in the Markdown output, corrupting table formatting and section spacing.

Fix: The proposal should explicitly state that all Go template files must use {{- and -}} trim markers on all block-opening and block-closing lines, and that output parity tests (AC7) must verify generated content against a known-good reference output, not just 'contains expected sections'.

**[MINOR, confidence 75]: The last(i, length) FuncMap pattern requires explicit length threading**

The Jinja2 template uses {{ loop.last }} transparently (Jinja2 injects it). The Go equivalent last(i, length int) bool requires the template to know its slice length before the range loop. This forces a pattern like:

    {{- $n := len .Items -}}
    {{- range $i, $item := .Items -}}
    {{- if not (last $i $n) }}, {{ end -}}

This is workable but more error-prone than Jinja2's implicit loop.last. The proposal should note this pattern explicitly in the template design section so workers don't discover it mid-implementation.

No impact on correctness if addressed during implementation — this is a template ergonomics note for the implementation team.

**[MINOR, confidence 65]: Figure YAML loading not mentioned in go:embed scope**

The proposal embeds templates via //go:embed templates/*.go.tmpl but does not address figure YAML loading. The Python gen_skills.py calls _load_figure_content() at generation time, reading from skills/protocol/figures/*.yaml. Since go generate runs in the source tree, runtime filesystem access to those YAMLs is fine. But the proposal should clarify whether figures are: (a) loaded from filesystem at go generate time (fine, no embed needed), or (b) embedded alongside templates (would need //go:embed for figures too). As written it is ambiguous — the implementation team may make divergent choices.

---

### Elegance Assessment

The package layout is idiomatic Go: internal/codegen/ for the library, tools/codegen/ for the //go:generate binary, with clear file→type ownership. specs.go + specs_data.go separation (types vs data literals) is clean and matches the Go convention of separating struct definitions from their initialization data. The GenerateOptions struct pattern is proportionate — it captures exactly the three caller-controlled behaviors (Diff, Write, Init) without over-engineering. go:embed for templates eliminates filesystem coupling at generation time and is the right approach. The FuncMap covers the template needs (join, slug, last, lower) with the caveat about last() noted above.

The IMPORTANT finding (whitespace trim_blocks gap) is a known Go text/template limitation that will affect output fidelity if not handled at implementation time. It does not block the plan — it is a constraint the implementor must be aware of. The proposal can proceed with this noted.

VOTE: ACCEPT

author: David Huu Pham
text:
REVIEW-C-1 (Elegance axis) — Reviewer C

## Summary

Reviewed all files under internal/codegen/ and tools/codegen/main.go against the elegance axis. Findings are MINOR across the board — no BLOCKERs, no IMPORTANT items.

Severity groups: BLOCKER=aura-plugins-p713o (empty), IMPORTANT=aura-plugins-hdk6j (empty), MINOR=aura-plugins-qggo2

---

## BLOCKER findings: none

## IMPORTANT findings: none

## MINOR findings

### MINOR-1: Dual template-loading strategies within the same package

Location: internal/codegen/skills.go (lines 26-30, 108-117) vs internal/codegen/agents.go (line 75-84)

What: skills.go embeds two templates as raw string variables via individual //go:embed directives, then passes them to mustParseTemplate(name, src string). agents.go uses ParseFS against the directory-level embed.FS declared in embed.go.

Why it matters to end users: Inconsistency makes it harder for contributors to add new generators — the pattern to follow is ambiguous. A new engineer adding a fourth generator must choose between two styles.

Why it is MINOR not IMPORTANT: Both strategies compile and work correctly. The current behaviour and output are not affected. The embed.FS approach (agents.go) is the more idiomatic and extensible path since it does not require a new package-level string variable per template.

Suggested fix: Unify on the ParseFS approach. Remove skillHeaderTmpl and skillSubFigureTmpl string variables, remove the _ "embed" import from skills.go (it is only needed for the string-embed directives), and convert mustParseTemplate to load from templatesFS via ParseFS the same way agents.go does. The mustParseTemplate helper itself remains valid as a panic-on-error convenience wrapper; it just changes its input from a string to a parsed *template.Template loaded from the FS.

---

### MINOR-2: path.Dir re-implemented inline in GenerateAgent

Location: internal/codegen/agents.go lines 174-179

What: The parent directory extraction uses strings.LastIndex(agentPath, "/") inline rather than filepath.Dir, which is already imported indirectly (path/filepath is available in the module and used in tools/codegen/main.go).

Why: filepath.Dir is the idiomatic Go way to extract the parent directory of a path, handles edge cases (root path, Windows separators) and is already present as a standard library import.

Suggested fix: Replace lines 174-179 with:
  dir := filepath.Dir(agentPath)
  if dir != "." {
      os.MkdirAll(dir, 0o755)
  }

---

### MINOR-3: GenerateOptions.Init semantics not applicable to GenerateAgent but not enforced

Location: internal/codegen/agents.go doc comment (line 131), agents.go GenerateAgent signature

What: The doc comment says 'opts.Init is not used by GenerateAgent (agents are fully generated)' but this is only documented, not enforced. A caller passing Init=true gets silent no-op behaviour.

Why it matters: From an API elegance perspective, accepting a field and silently ignoring it surprises callers. The note in the doc comment partially mitigates this but the contract is loose.

Suggested fix (MINOR — not urgent): Consider a separate AgentGenerateOptions that omits the Init field entirely, keeping the type surface honest. Alternatively, a comment inside the function body is sufficient if the team wants to avoid a new type.

---

### MINOR-4: Workflow block duplicated identically in skill_header.go.tmpl and agent_definition.go.tmpl

Location: internal/codegen/templates/skill_header.go.tmpl (lines 144-178) and templates/agent_definition.go.tmpl (lines 63-87)

What: Both templates render the Workflows section with nearly identical stage/action/exit-condition structure. The agent template uses ## headings where the skill template uses ####, but the inner loop logic is a copy.

Why it is MINOR: Go text/template does not support includes in a lightweight way without template.ParseFiles multi-template composition, and unifying them would require a shared partial template file plus ParseFiles chains. The current duplication is explicit and readable. Worth noting for a future pass if templates grow.

Suggested fix: Accept as-is for MVP. If workflows section grows significantly, extract a shared _workflows.go.tmpl partial rendered via {{template "workflows" .}} with a nested define block.

---

## Vote: ACCEPT

The package layout is idiomatic Go. File organisation (one concern per file: specs, context, markers, skills, agents, schema, embed) is clean and each file has a clear responsibility. GenerateOptions is declared once and shared. Error messages follow C-actionable-errors consistently across all generators. The embed.FS design intent (avoid duplicate go:embed declarations) is sound; it is only partially realised because skills.go still uses string-embed for two templates (MINOR-1 above). The unifiedDiff helper is straightforward and appropriately scoped. The tools/codegen/main.go entry point is clean, linear, and fail-slow (collects all errors before exiting).

The four MINOR findings above are all low-urgency polish items that do not affect correctness, testability, or end-user output. No BLOCKER or IMPORTANT findings.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
REVIEW-B CODEGEN-REVIEW-B-1 (Test Quality axis)
Round: 1
Reviewer task: aura-plugins-vjv50
BLOCKER group: aura-plugins-z0mny (CLOSED — no blockers)
IMPORTANT group: aura-plugins-zfjiw
MINOR group: aura-plugins-wgdbq

## Overview

All 7 slices reviewed. Tests pass (91.3% statement coverage). All 5 fixture files present. Vote at end.

---

## Criterion 1: YAML fixtures via testutil.LoadFixtures — PASS

All five FixtureName constants added to testutil/fixtures.go: CodegenMarkers, CodegenContext, CodegenAgents, CodegenSkills, CodegenSchema. Every fixture-driven test uses testutil.LoadFixtures with the typed constant. The proposal review (PROPOSAL-2-REVIEW-B-1) noted this as a non-blocking observation — it is fully implemented.

specs_test.go does NOT use YAML fixtures — it directly calls the public spec maps (PhaseSpecs, RoleSpecs, ConstraintSpecs, etc.). This is correct: specs_test.go tests static data completeness, not behavior that varies. Using YAML fixtures to test static data maps would add indirection without benefit.

---

## Criterion 2: Test boundaries (public API, not internals) — PASS

All test files are in package codegen_test (black-box). Internal helpers (renderHeader, renderSubSkillHeader, renderAgent, commandsForRole, etc.) are not tested directly. Tests call only: FindMarkerPositions, HasMarkers, ReplaceMarkerRegion, PrependMarkers, GenerateSkill, GenerateSubSkill, GenerateAgent, GenerateSchema, GetRoleContext, GetPhaseContext, ConstraintToRoleRefs, ConstraintToPhaseRefs, and the public spec maps.

The splitKeepEnds helper in markers_test.go is a test-local utility, not a call into implementation internals.

---

## Criterion 3: Error paths coverage — PASS WITH ONE IMPORTANT FINDING

All 6 MarkerError cases are covered in markers.yaml: missing_both_markers, missing_begin, missing_end, duplicate_begin, duplicate_end, reversed_markers. TestFindMarkerPositions_PathInError verifies the path appears in the error message (actionable-errors).

ReplaceMarkerRegion error propagation tested in TestReplaceMarkerRegion_MalformedReturnsError.

GenerateSkill error paths covered: missing markers (returns *MarkerError), unknown role (not-found error), Init mode (prepends markers then succeeds).

IMPORTANT FINDING: GenerateSubSkill has 61.9% coverage. The Init mode for GenerateSubSkill (opts.Init + missing markers) is not tested — skills_test.go only tests Init=true for GenerateSkill (TestGenerateSkill_InitMode), not for GenerateSubSkill. Given that GenerateSubSkill has its own Init path that differs (Write mode interacts with marker prepend), this is an untested public behavior. It mirrors a production code path (--init flag on sub-skill files). Recorded as IMPORTANT.

IMPORTANT FINDING: GenerateSchemaToFile (the file-writing variant of GenerateSchema) has 0.0% coverage. This is a public function (exported) and the one actually called by tools/codegen/main.go in production. The tests only call GenerateSchema(io.Writer), not GenerateSchemaToFile. The Write=true path (which creates files) is entirely untested at the integration boundary. Recorded as IMPORTANT.

GenerateAgent error path for unknown role covered in TestGenerateAgent_SectionChecks (invalid role in fixture returns error). However GenerateAgent's Write path is tested by TestGenerateAgent_WritesToDisk — good.

---

## Criterion 4: 'Contains expected sections' vs exact string matching — PASS

skill tests: MustContain entries are semantic tokens ('C-worker-gates', 'Owned Phases', 'Handoffs') not full paragraphs. No snapshot/full-string comparison.

agents tests: Same pattern — structural sections ('## Constraints', '## Behaviors') plus constraint IDs.

TestGenerateAgent_SupervisorContainsSections uses Contains not Equal for all assertions.

One note: TestGenerateSkill_WriteMode and TestGenerateAgent_WritesToDisk use assert.Equal to compare returned string to disk content. This is appropriate — these tests verify I/O correctness not template output, so exact equality is correct there.

---

## Criterion 5: Public API surface coverage — PASS

All public functions tested:
- FindMarkerPositions: fixture-driven + path-in-error test
- HasMarkers: 4 inline tests (both, begin-only, end-only, neither)
- ReplaceMarkerRegion: 5 inline tests (drop-prefix, preserve-prefix, no-body, malformed, missing-newline)
- PrependMarkers: 4 tests (no markers, idempotent, HasMarkers roundtrip, init-mode roundtrip)
- GenerateSkill: section checks, missing markers, init mode, write mode, dry-run, unknown role, figures dir, template-owns-begin, body-preserved
- GenerateSubSkill: section checks, preserves-prefix, missing-markers, unknown-command
- GenerateSchema: valid XML, XML declaration, root element, all roles, all phases, constraint role-refs, phase-refs, CDATA in code, all constraints, all handoffs, all sections, pretty-printed
- GetRoleContext: constraint sets (fixture), commands, handoffs, phases, role spec fields, review axes, all roles valid
- GetPhaseContext: labels, transitions, constraint sets (fixture), general constraints, all phases valid
- ConstraintToRoleRefs / ConstraintToPhaseRefs: inverse mappings with role-specific and general constraint checks

Gap: GenerateSchemaToFile — 0% coverage (IMPORTANT, see above).

---

## Criterion 6: Missing test cases — minor gaps only

MINOR: context_test.go uses must_contain / must_not_contain lists but not exact set equality. The proposal review noted 'subset vs exact set' — the implementation uses MustContain + MustNotContain which is a practical approximation. The fixture for 'reviewer' has a 11-item must_contain and 6-item must_not_contain, which is thorough, but it is theoretically possible to have extra unexpected constraints injected. Adding an ExactCount field to the fixture or a separate test asserting len(ctx.Constraints) == expected_count would be stronger. Recorded as MINOR.

MINOR: skills.yaml sub_skill_cases has only 1 sub-skill case (cmd-sup-plan). The Python gen_skills.py generates sub-skills for multiple commands. A second fixture case for a reviewer or worker sub-command would improve regression protection. Recorded as MINOR.

MINOR: markers.yaml has 3 happy-path cases and 6 error cases. Missing: a case with Windows-style CRLF line endings, which splitKeepEnds would need to handle if SKILL.md files are ever edited on Windows. Not a current concern but worth noting.

---

## Vote

ACCEPT

The test suite is well-structured, correctly uses YAML fixtures through the typed FixtureName pattern, covers all 6 MarkerError error cases, and applies the contains-expected-sections strategy throughout. The two IMPORTANT findings (GenerateSubSkill Init path uncovered at 61.9%, GenerateSchemaToFile at 0.0%) are coverage gaps in public functions, not test strategy failures — they should be addressed in a follow-up epic or before p11-impl-uat. No BLOCKERs. Confidence: high (90%).

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT — Reviewer C (Elegance axis)

Package layout is idiomatic Go with clear single-responsibility files. GenerateOptions is declared once and properly shared. Error messages satisfy C-actionable-errors throughout. The embed.FS design (embed.go) correctly centralises the directory embed, and the individual string-embeds in skills.go are a MINOR inconsistency (aura-plugins-c5zlu) not a defect. Four MINOR findings filed (aura-plugins-c5zlu, aura-plugins-oprzr, aura-plugins-57s4q, aura-plugins-526n6) — all are polish items suitable for the follow-up epic. No BLOCKERs, no IMPORTANT items. text: REVIEW-A (Correctness): Starting code review. Severity groups created: BLOCKER=aura-plugins-qox4m, IMPORTANT=aura-plugins-7do5v, MINOR=aura-plugins-8f744. Reviewing all 7 slices.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT

Reviewer B — Test Quality axis
Review task: aura-plugins-vjv50
Round: 1
Confidence: 90%

## Summary

No BLOCKERs. Two IMPORTANT findings, two MINOR findings.

IMPORTANT findings (do not block ACCEPT; should be addressed in follow-up):
- aura-plugins-sq11q: GenerateSchemaToFile at 0.0% coverage — add TestGenerateSchemaToFile_WriteMode to schema_test.go
- aura-plugins-9wpwf: GenerateSubSkill Init mode untested (61.9%) — add TestGenerateSubSkill_InitMode mirroring TestGenerateSkill_InitMode

MINOR findings:
- aura-plugins-4r9ms: context constraint tests use must_contain/must_not_contain, not exact count — add count assertion for stable roles
- aura-plugins-8k1s8: skills.yaml has only 1 sub_skill_case — add 1-2 more fixture entries

## Rationale

The test suite correctly applies the contains-expected-sections strategy throughout (not exact string matching), uses YAML fixtures consistently via the typed FixtureName constants, covers all 6 MarkerError cases, maintains black-box package boundaries (codegen_test), and achieves 91.3% overall statement coverage. The public API surface (FindMarkerPositions, HasMarkers, ReplaceMarkerRegion, PrependMarkers, GenerateSkill, GenerateSubSkill, GenerateAgent, GenerateSchema, GetRoleContext, GetPhaseContext, ConstraintToRoleRefs, ConstraintToPhaseRefs) is tested. The two IMPORTANT gaps are in exported functions that the production go:generate pipeline calls directly, so they should be closed before or during p11-impl-uat.

author: David Huu Pham

All constraint mappings in context.go correctly mirror Python _ROLE_CONSTRAINTS and _PHASE_CONSTRAINTS. The role and phase constraint sets match entry-for-entry including the authoring rationale comments. The schema.xml structure is correct with all required sections (enums, labels, review-axes, phases, roles, commands, handoffs, constraints, task-titles, documents, dependency-model, followup-lifecycle, procedure-steps, checklists, coordination-commands, workflows, figures). The text/template port correctly implements Jinja2 trim_blocks parity using {{- -}} whitespace trimming. The go:generate entry point in tools/codegen/main.go wires all 4 generators correctly. Tests pass (nix develop --command go test ./internal/codegen/...).

No BLOCKERs found.

IMPORTANT findings (2):
- aura-plugins-638zy: PhaseComplete uses copyConstraints(generalConstraints) but Python uses frozenset() (empty). The deviation is arguably intentional but undocumented as such. Tests accommodate both behaviours.
- aura-plugins-yuxg2: embed.go claims to be the single shared FS but skills.go maintains its own //go:embed raw strings for 2 of 3 templates, contradicting the comment.

End-user impact: The generated SKILL.md and schema.xml outputs are correct and structurally faithful to the Python originals. The PhaseComplete deviation and embed split are internal implementation concerns that do not affect generated output quality.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
Architect review complete (2026-03-13). Key refinements applied:

1. CDATA sentinel strategy CORRECTED: Original proposal stored __CDATA_START__code__CDATA_END__ in xml chardata fields, which would cause encoding/xml to HTML-escape the code content within the sentinels (e.g. < → <), producing malformed CDATA. Revised to Option A: keep buildConstraints + buildProcedureSteps (the 2 of 17 sections that need CDATA) as manual fmt.Fprintf. Convert other 15 sections to encoding/xml struct marshalling. No sentinel needed.

2. Validator parser changed from streaming decoder to generic XMLNode tree: The Python validator uses root.iter(), root.find(), root.findAll() (tree navigation). Streaming requires 2+ passes with context tracking. Generic XMLNode via encoding/xml Unmarshal is cleaner and closer to the Python model. Removed etree dependency from ValidateTree signature — now uses *XMLNode.

3. SLICE-B scope clarified: Only 15 of 17 sections need conversion. buildConstraints and buildProcedureSteps stay manual (2 lines of CDATA each). This reduces SLICE-B risk significantly.

4. Acceptance criteria expanded: Added validator error contract (error = IO/parse only, violations in []ValidationError), malformed XML handling (Structural layer error, no panic), validator independence constraint (must not import codegen schema types), round-trip test specification.

5. Fixture header targets specified: Concrete header strings for skills.yaml (## Constraints (Given/When/Then/Should Not), ## Handoffs, etc.) and agents.yaml figure assertions.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE — Reviewer A (Correctness axis)

## What passes

1. **CDATA audit is correct**: Lines 1087 and 1509 are the only two CDATA sites (buildConstraints, buildProcedureSteps). Option A (keep 2 manual, convert 15) is technically sound and zero-risk for escaping.
2. **XMLNode generic tree approach**: xml:",any,attr" + xml:",any" + xml:",chardata" is the idiomatic Go pattern for tree-oriented XML navigation matching Python's root.iter()/find()/findAll(). No etree dependency. ✓
3. **Validator independence constraint**: Hard constraint stated clearly — no import of codegen types. ✓
4. **All 3 UAT revisions addressed**: UAT-REV-1 → SLICE-A+B, UAT-REV-2 → SLICE-A+C, UAT-REV-3 → SLICE-D. ✓
5. **Round-trip test**: TestGenerateSchema_RoundTripValidation specified. ✓

## Issues requiring revision

### Issue 1 — Semantic rule count is wrong (BLOCKER: misleads workers)

The proposal states 'Port all 14 semantic rules from Python validate_schema.py'.

**Actual count**: The Python check_semantics implements exactly **12 rules**, numbered 1-6 then 9-14. Rules 7 and 8 are absent from the Python code (the comment numbering skips them). Workers handed this proposal will either:
  (a) search for 14 rules and be confused why only 12 exist, or
  (b) try to invent/implement rules 7 and 8 that do not exist, adding unspecified behaviour.

**Fix**: Change 'Port all 14 semantic rules' → 'Port the 12 implemented semantic rules (numbered 1-6 and 9-14 in validate_schema.py lines 529-696; rules 7-8 were planned but not implemented in Python).'

### Issue 2 — Error contract is internally contradictory (BLOCKER: worker will implement one of two incompatible semantics)

The ValidateSchema interface description and acceptance criterion give opposite answers about malformed XML:

- Interface description: 'error return is for IO/parse failure only; violations returned in []ValidationError'
- Acceptance criterion: 'Given malformed XML input to ValidateSchema when called then returns Structural layer error in **[]ValidationError** should never panic or return (nil, nil)'

The Python model (validate(), lines 711-723) returns parse errors as a ValidationError{layer=STRUCTURAL} in the list with no exception raised. The acceptance criterion matches the Python. But the interface description says parse failure goes in error — contradicting both the AC and the Python.

A worker will either:
  (a) Put XML parse errors in error (following interface description) — AC test fails
  (b) Put XML parse errors in []ValidationError (following AC + Python) — interface description is misleading

**Fix**: Resolve which semantics is authoritative, then make both consistent. Recommended: align with Python — only io.Reader read errors go in error; XML parse errors go in []ValidationError as Structural. Update the interface description to: 'error return covers only io.Reader read failures (e.g. os.ErrNotExist). XML parse errors are returned as ValidationError{Layer: Structural, ...} in the slice.'

## Minor (non-blocking, suggest address in description)

- SLICE-A says 'structs for all 17 sections' but Option A keeps 2 manual. Should ConstraintsSection and ProcedureStepsSection types be defined in SLICE-A or excluded? Workers splitting across slices need a clear answer.

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: REVISE — Reviewer B (Test Quality)

Three actionable issues that prevent safe implementation:

## Issue 1 (BLOCKER): New fixture keys silently ignored without code changes

SLICE-D adds two new YAML keys that don't exist in current fixture structs:
- skills.yaml:  — but skillRoleCase struct only has MustContain
- schema.yaml:  — but schemaConstraintCheck has no such field

Go's yaml.v3 unmarshalling silently ignores unknown struct fields. Workers adding these keys to YAML fixtures will see zero test failures AND zero test coverage — a false green.

Fix: Either (a) specify that these are additions to the existing  lists (no code changes needed — worker just adds items), or (b) explicitly require SLICE-D to add the struct fields and test iteration logic. Currently ambiguous.

## Issue 2 (REVISE): TestValidateSchema_MalformedXML missing from SLICE-C/D

The acceptance criterion states: 'Given malformed XML input to ValidateSchema when called then returns Structural layer error in []ValidationError should never panic or return (nil, nil).'

This is the validator error contract — arguably the most important behavioral guarantee for the new SLICE-C implementation. But no test is listed for it in SLICE-C or SLICE-D.

The ValidateTree path (which skips io.Reader parsing) also needs a test covering malformed-at-tree-level input.

Fix: Add TestValidateSchema_MalformedXML and TestValidateTree_MalformedInput to SLICE-C deliverables.

## Issue 3 (REVISE): Round-trip test should assert per-layer, not just total=0

TestGenerateSchema_RoundTripValidation asserts '0 errors.' When this test fails in future regressions, the layer that broke is unknown without re-running the validator manually. The validator already returns ErrorLayer-tagged ValidationErrors.

Fix: Assert per-layer counts:
  structuralErrors = filter by Layer==Structural → assert len==0
  referentialErrors = filter by Layer==Referential → assert len==0
  semanticErrors = filter by Layer==Semantic → assert len==0

This costs two extra assertions and provides dramatically better diagnostic output.

---

Summary: The round-trip + CDATA + header strategy is sound. The above issues risk SLICE-D producing dead test assertions (Issue 1) and leaving a core acceptance criterion untested (Issue 2). REVISE to address these before implementation.

All three round-1 blockers resolved:

1. Semantic rule count: now correctly states '12 implemented rules (numbered 1-6 and 9-14; rules 7-8 absent)'. Workers will port exactly what Python implements.

2. Error contract: interface definition and both acceptance criteria entries are now fully consistent. io.Reader failure → (nil, error); XML parse failure → ([]ValidationError{Structural}, nil); violations → ([]ValidationError{...}, nil); valid → (nil, nil).

author: David Huu Pham
text:
VOTE: ACCEPT — Reviewer B (Test Quality)

All three round-1 findings addressed correctly:

1. Fix B1 (struct fields): MustContainHeaders, MustHaveFigureBlocks, MustHaveCDATACode explicitly required with corresponding test loops. Silent YAML key risk is closed.

2. Fix B2 (TestValidateSchema_MalformedXML): Fully specified — input '<aura-protocol><unclosed', assert error==nil, len(errs)>=1, errs[0].Layer==Structural. Consistent with the Fix A2 error contract.

3. Fix B3 (per-layer round-trip): TestGenerateSchema_RoundTripValidation now asserts structural==0, referential==0, semantic==0 with actionable failure messages naming the layer.

Bonus: Fix A2 error contract is cleaner than PROPOSAL-3 — io.Reader failures return (nil, error), XML parse failures return ([]ValidationError{Structural}, nil). The new AC for io.Reader failures ('should never silently swallow IO errors') completes the contract coverage.

Test strategy is sound end-to-end: round-trip integration + CDATA anchored to C-agent-commit + header assertions + malformed XML contract test + per-layer diagnostics.
